review_text:
20 Small Mazes was refreshing because 1 hour of mazes was a nice change of pace. I really liked that game and played it twice.

Strange Jigsaws was 3 hours. And some of the puzzles were a bit too obtuse. Overall it was more draining than refreshing.
This game is too weird without any guidance so not really fun playing. Figure it out yourself is ok but this is annoying.
I wish I could recommend it; it's a fun concept and I can see that it has some great ideas. Unfortunately, I've tried to play it twice, and both times it's hung after around 15 minutes.
Some puzzles aren't bad but I didn't enjoy the game at all.
cute game but it sucks

I have been a big fan of Freedom Planet and Freedom Planet 2. I played this game and maybe I didn't play long enough, but considering the success and level of detail put into FP and FP2, this game goes nowhere close to those. Galaxy Trail's biggest hit was FP and they did nothing to market the second installation of the sequel and the future is not looking bright. Their other games Petal Crash 1, 2 and Grapple Force Rena are games that sold horribly and have niche audiences. I have no reason to want to play this game any further, the music is gaudy and the visuals are not impressive.

upd: The bug MIGHT be fixed now in the latest patch but I can't personally confirm it yet--the patch also has introduced several game design decisions I strongly disagree with, making playing the current "endgame" incredibly unfun. I hope and trust the devs will be able to fix some of the glaring issues in the next major update, but for now I once again can't recommend the game--it isn't what I've enjoyed playing previously.

Despite the creator advertising this as "on par with Celeste or Pizza Tower" or "the most underrated game on Steam", this truly is not a good game. Don't let the "very positive" feedback fool you as if there's one thing I've learned since using this platform: Just about any nostalgia-inducing game gets at least "very positive" feedback since nostalgia blindness is very prevalent in our current culture.

Despite its aesthetics, Garbanzo Quest completely forgets what made the games it's trying to emulate great. Instead of actually crafting an engaging challenge in the level design, this game's design philosophy boils down to "spam enemies until the playtesters struggled". This makes the game hard, sure, but hard in a frustrating way rather than a fun way. This definitely isn't a skill issue either, since I'm someone who beat the original Castlevania, and even the original Ninja Gaiden on real hardware with no cheats. It's not that I feel as though I couldn't beat this game, I just don't want to invest the effort because I don't find it fun.

The design philosophy isn't where this game's problems end either, just the main problem. The art in this game isn't even that great to begin with, looking worse than the average actual NES game in my opinion, but my bigger issue is the lack of variety in the level art. World after world is snow themed, and even changing palettes a little more would have done wonders for these backgrounds, but this combined with too many levels relative to original ideas added up to often feeling as if I was just playing the same level over and over. The constant walls of destructible blocks are also extremely annoying and didn't really add anything to the gameplay aside from slowing you down and making you hold down the fire button. As if repetitive designs weren't bad enough, the destructible blocks made it so this game didn't even have good pacing to get you through these dull sections.

Also, the hub where your friends stay is annoying and awkward to navigate with seemingly random twists, turns, and pits, when it could have just been one giant room. I got more tired of that place each time I visited.

If you're nostalgic for Super Mario World, you'd do better with a Super Mario World romhack than this, and those are free.

This game is beautiful looking, the graphics and animations are awesome.  The music is nice, the game runs well, and the controls are perfectly fine.  As someone who loves the clay aesthetic, this should have been right up my alley.  Sadly, while the graphics and music are good, the gameplay really suffers.  

I know this game is a remake, and seems to be very faithful.  I do applaud them for that.  However, as far as shmups go, this game is excruciating.  Stages are long and drawn out, enemy patterns repeat far too often with little variation, powerups are set to a timer so you cannot take any with you and strategize your play.  

It's sad to me, because this game looks so awesome and you can tell they put a lot of great amazing work into it.  If you are a beginner, have kids, or are nostalgic for the original then this is probably a good game to pick up.  For those of us looking for a bit more, this is a skip.

It seems like you have to have grown up on the old resident evil games to enjoy this.
The aiming system for the guns is absolute dogshit, I don't know how you could enjoy using it, plus it takes so much ammo to kill something, but ammo is very sparse, and running away just makes you lose a shit ton of health because enemies will just lunge at you, and you just have to use all of your supplies to heal.
There's an exploration mode, but I usually prefer to play these games the intended way, so I chose the survival horror mode. But it's just too unforgiving, it's not really a challenge at all it's just unfair- but maybe if you're used to playing with shitty controls you can play it and enjoy it, but to me it's way too much effort to try and deal with janky, unintuitive controls to play the game, like seriously whenever I try to aim my gun it goes in a random direction and disorients me, so while I scramble to move my camera to the right position, I get hit by the enemies, also the aiming is slow as fuck which adds even more to the difficulty- and, like I said, it takes a million bullets to finally kill something. This sucks for times when you are required to fight off a monster. 
Another reason I wouldn't want to do the exploration mode is because at that point if the game isn't fun to play, I'll just watch someone do a playthrough, because I do not really enjoy walking simulators on my own, I'd get much more enjoyment watching someone play it than just go through the tedium of exploring. 
I'm not even saying it's a bad game, I'm mostly just saying it's not for me. Maybe if you're nostalgic for oldschool survival horror, you'll like this- but I personally have no attachment to it. So, if anything I said here are things you don't like in games, don't buy it. Even half-priced I still refunded it, but honestly I wouldn't have known it wasn't for me if I had never at least tried it, cause, like I said, I never grew up with games like this.

There's clearly a lot of care and love that went into this game. The folks who made it love old school survival horror and it shows. However, I didn't have fun playing it. Old survival horror has the occasional puzzle but this game is very puzzle heavy. And a lot of time you have to go through one end of the area to the other. Backtracking is a way of life for this game. The biggest problem with this is that the enemies respawn and get worse. You get the occasional new area but mostly you're trying to remember where that puzzle was that you finally found the item for. There are a few ways to change the difficulty, turning off monsters entirely and there's a hint system so the developers have done some ways to mitigate the issues. However, I would rather not change the way the game is meant to be played and instead I just watched the second half on Youtube. The story is okay but fairly basic. There's no huge surprises. It's not horrible by any means, if puzzles are your favourite part of survival horror and you can deal with clunky aiming and monsters that just keep coming back, then give this game a try. But if that doesn't sound like you, I'd just say give it a pass.

[h1]Fun for an hour, but you'll eventually drown in benches.[/h1] [i]A cute city builder that starts strong but becomes a repetitive chore. Once you've placed your 40th garden and 200th street lamp, the magic disappears. Great for a quick distraction, but don't expect a deep experience.[/i]

[h1]The Bench-Placing Simulator[/h1]

[b]A Charming Start[/b] At first, I really enjoyed this game. There is something very satisfying about planning where people will live and decorating their homes with trees, fences, and gardens to keep them happy. I even went as far as making 40 individual gardens—one for every single house!

[b]The "Copy-Paste" Problem[/b] However, the charm wears off quickly. You eventually realize you're just placing the same street lamps and benches every two meters. Even when the second map introduces new mechanics that force you back to your first city, it just feels like the same cycle repeating itself. I got bored quite fast.

[b]Verdict[/b] It’s a cute game for a couple of hours, but it lacks the depth to keep you interested long-term. If you’ve seen one garden, you’ve seen them all.

[b]Score: 4/10 — I never want to see a street lamp again.[/b]

This game wasnt terrible but I cant say its good either the placing is a bit glitchy and no it is not my pc I have insanely good specs also there isnt enough items not enough buildings and I know this is just a early access but that does not mean it will be good later on once it gets better I will change my review.
Still in early access and not much to do in game. It's like a super chill town building game, but it's just that. No challenges or surprises, so I guess maybe some people like it.
Great game when it doesn't crash, however after about 20 mins without fail it shuts down my PC completely.

Great bones. Problem is I want to spend my time making ALL my townfolk happy, not just the rich townfolk. The concept of the Bourgeoisie being a special class I have to change all my design plans around to be happy stinks. I don't want to spend my free time playing a game where I have constantly be a yes man in service of rich people, I already have a real life. It evokes real emotional disdain in the game for me. Give me a power fantasy where I can treat all my citizens by their needs and desires instead of their financial power. Encourage me to build neighborhoods for nature folks, and city folks, and sunflower folks, and blue house folks, and ponds folks, and beach folks, etc. "I have more money so I matter more and now your town planning is screwed up" is such a lame way to throw a cozy city builder off a cliff. (Devs, this is a chill game. Maybe think, "what will the player be excited to do" instead of "what annoyances can we force onto the player" when it comes to the research tree and this "Bourgeoisie" theme/mechanic.)

A huge part of the game seems to be towns/citys integrated with each others, but be aware there is bugs with save files. You may spend several hours in one town, just to lose the town if you start the next town.

The bug has been reported several times. Game braking to me at least. Can't recommend until they find a solution.

People really seem to love this game, and honestly I'm torn because at its core, I also love this game.  I think it has a beautiful artistic style, I think its approach to the genre is interesting and fun.  But I also really found it pretty boring after a short while, and it became obvious that the "progression" was just going to be a lot of rinse and repeat without much in the way of advancement or real achievement.  People want things, you give them those things, over and over and over.  Is this a core mechanic of this genre?  Sure.  But the way this game goes about it is just... dull.

I know it's meant to be relaxing, and a lot of the reviews emphasize how chill it is, but honestly I found it rather directionless.  This family needs a house with trees?  Plop down some trees.  Hooray, now they're happy.  This family wants water nearby?  Plop a house next to some water.  Hooray, they're happy now.  And while this is all simple and well and good for it, it just gets... boring.  And tedious.  And repetitive.  And not in the fun way a lot of games in this bracket can make it.  Again, I know this is meant to be a "relaxing" approach to a "city building" game, and it is definitely that.  And if you're someone who just wants to have a pretty game to look at on the side with no real investment in the development of your town beyond "I planted some flowers and now they're happier", then by all means, this is the game for you.

Honestly, judging by the reviews, this is obviously the game for a vast majority of the people playing this.  Which is honestly a bit confusing and maybe a little concerning, to consider the bar is apparently so low for thousands of people.  But, I grasp that I'm in the minority here, and very probably not the intended audience for this game.  And again, I don't think this is a "bad" game, it's just nothing impressive, especially not for $25 in Early Access.  There are plenty of other games in this genre that are in the same state of completion, if not further (or even released) for the same price or a few dollars more.  They'll give you 10x the content, engagement, and sense of accomplishment that this game will.  But if you don't want those things, and you instead just want to plop a few things down and feel like you've "built a town" then by all means, buy this and never look back.

Maybe I'll come back to this someday when there's more content.  I picked it up as soon as it came out because I loved the concept and the look of it, and I'm all for supporting smaller devs, but it just felt shallow, hollow, lackluster.  Pretty to look at, ugly to play.  And I returned it almost instantly, because the mundane nature of the whole thing was quickly apparent, and I didn't want to miss that 2 hour window.

Very beautiful city-builder, nicely using voxel graphics to allow micro-beautification of every building. 

That being said, at this point it has a terrible economy. Basically the more you grow, the more your pops demand. However, this game also uses workplace mechanics, so every building needs pops to function. In turn, the growth of your city is steadily decreasing in time, as for every new utility you need more pops to work in them, which requires you to employ more pops for previous needs. When games like Anno grants you more pops for satisfying more needs, this game punishes you for expanding. I would say it takes twice as much time to reach each next city update. It makes the campaign a tedious experience, opposite to snowballing.

I thought this game was just about cozy town building with less limitations than other games in this genre but actually this game currently having more limitation than production games. I tried to build my cozy town but i couldnt as you can understand earlier that what i said. Because houses needs stalls really really near them. Maybe it is only a problem for me but if i wanted to build a practical town instead of an cozy one, ı would just play another game instead.

i understand that this is still in early access, but i feel that this feedback is valid. 

it was very cute and fun at first, but became very tedious and grindy, and i'm just about at 9 hours now. all of the game goals start becoming very much the same thing over and over and it doesn't actually add anything to the gameplay, nothing feels like an accomplishment or like i'm doing anything gratifying. it feels like an idle mobile game at this point.

i also have not had any new asks from the villagers in a while, which gave me something to do. there really isn't anything to do besides decorate at this point. you can't interact with the villagers at all unless they ask you for something. after a few hours you don't contribute anything to the town in a way that feels fun. you unlock more decorations and buildings, put them down, and then literally nothing else happens to make it seem like it has an impact on the game. 

the art and concept, and sound design is very good (i like how getting close to certain buildings makes unique sound effects), i am just surprised at how i have lost interest in this game so quickly. 

if it actually had an effect on the villagers to put certain buildings or roads down, or having a certain layout for the town, that would be more fun. also i don't like how upgrading the roads makes them all look the same. being able to tell more of a story and make a better looking town by having roads of different styles was nicer. if upgrading the roads kept them looking the same that would be good.

this is also advertised at being able to crowd buildings, but eventually villagers get upset at too much crowding... so there's no way to make a really interesting crowded city without constant frowny bubbles from the villagers. i hope thye fix that as well and make most of them have a trait to like being in a big city or something

I have multiple gripes with this game to be completely honest.
Relaxing city builders are exactly what I love, next to RPGs, rogue-lite, and some other genres.
And overall, I really like this game.

However there are some things I can just not put up with at this point in time:

1) Performance: Yes it is still in EA, but as soon as I crossed the 400 inhabitants threshold the games performance began to drop significantly. From short freezes when placing new buildings, over longer freezes when moving shops, etc. It eventually got less and less playable, the closer I got to 600 inhabitants.
Which, EA or not, meant I could not enjoy playing it any more and was forced to abandon the city and start anew, just to run into the same issue eventually.
And I got a fairly good setup too. 5080TI, 64 GB DDR5 @6200, full m.2 NVME (2x 990 @2TB), Ryzen 9 9700 X3D.

2) Micro- and resource-management: For a "cozy" and "relaxing" city builder, you have to be too cautious and aware about your resources, supply chains, distributions, supply-ranges, supply-leverages, "traits" (preferences), etc. The deeper you dive, the less of a cozy experience it becomes, and the more optimised everything needs to be. In particular, with how workforce relates to everything.
You want to grow your stage 2 population, but they require amenities that require stage 1 population.
but in order to sustain more stage 1 population, you have to also meet their needs and requirements which requires yet more stage 1 population.
At some point, I felt like I had to do more labourforce management, than in any of the Anno games.
Not exactly cozy or relaxing any more.

In particular that last gripe kind of ruined it for me. As I went in with the expectation of "cozy" and "relaxing" as advertised, but ended up really having to deal with micro and macro management of resourceflow, optimisation, etc.
While Sandbox was fun, it did also take away a lot of the fun that comes with watching your settlement reach milestones and grow more organically.

I definitely will return to it after some patches (performance and balance) and happily change my review as applicable.
But for now, it simply did not meet my personal expectations.

Which is where I have a gripe with Valve/Steam xD
I would still give it a Thumbs Up, cuz as a game it is great.
Just not according to what I expected, therefor I personally did not like it as much, which equals a Thumbs Down.

So, as a foot note:
I can absolutely recommend it for the price point of around 20 bucks.
Just personally did not meet my expectations according to its advertised core gameplay loop, as such I'll give a downvote (for now).

[h1]Fun for an hour, but you'll eventually drown in benches.[/h1] [i]A cute city builder that starts strong but becomes a repetitive chore. Once you've placed your 40th garden and 200th street lamp, the magic disappears. Great for a quick distraction, but don't expect a deep experience.[/i]

[h1]The Bench-Placing Simulator[/h1]

[b]A Charming Start[/b] At first, I really enjoyed this game. There is something very satisfying about planning where people will live and decorating their homes with trees, fences, and gardens to keep them happy. I even went as far as making 40 individual gardens—one for every single house!

[b]The "Copy-Paste" Problem[/b] However, the charm wears off quickly. You eventually realize you're just placing the same street lamps and benches every two meters. Even when the second map introduces new mechanics that force you back to your first city, it just feels like the same cycle repeating itself. I got bored quite fast.

[b]Verdict[/b] It’s a cute game for a couple of hours, but it lacks the depth to keep you interested long-term. If you’ve seen one garden, you’ve seen them all.

[b]Score: 4/10 — I never want to see a street lamp again.[/b]

but
the dialogue spoil everything to the point the game is a chore.

For one, there are two type sof dialogue, but most appens in huge modal visual novel boxes.
Those start automaticlly, second you can't skipp them and they are relatively slow if you already know them, you need to read them to figure out some puzlles, and lately, you can't save the game during dialogues, apparently, so you're forced to read everything and you can only move until the next dialogue steal your freedom of movement whenever you are ready for that, or focused enough to read it or not... little by little, that way, reading dialogues feel like a chor, and you start to try to avoid them, and ultimately, start to skip them all...wich makes them feel even more like a chore.

To be honest, It's very long-wined and let you little to no sense of freedom at all. Many choices are fake / just there to keep you guessing at what you should do to 'solve the puzzl".Puzzles are actually like visual novel ideas to figure out through dialogue, expect that the ideas have to be found my clicking on area on the side sscolling map like in a point-and-click game.  . 

So it's a visual novel, point-and-clicker 2D platformer on railroads with timed minigames... 
But though must give a bigger sense of freedom thank to having more than one type of gameplay, in this case, it's the reverse: whe the game shifts from platformer to visual novel without a warning, or from that to a timed minigame, it's just jarring, it breaks the flow. Nah no Dao dao in this game, though it tries.

And the only way to exit the game without Alt+F4 is through the Settings menu, not in the main mny, no in the save menu. Counterintuitive.

This game is definitly not what I thought. The cute decors aren't interactive like in modern side crolling adventures: they are P&C game backgrounds, and there is no mystery solving uless you count advencing dialogs and figuring our that a square fit a quare hole and click the square object on the map when the dialogue talks about a square... a puzzle.
For some reason I also though there was an AI character in the game, because of the console screen in the trailer. There isn't, not in 2h 8 of playing at least; 
I can't help but wonder how many positive evaluations are because furries got attached to the intresting, and great, to be honest, visual design, and the ok backstory, being overindulgent about the game flow because of that. 

Skipping through intempestive long winded blocking dialogue with empty times had grown into a punishment so I uninstalled. As someone wrote here in their evaluation: 
"Some people say to play for 3-5 hours more, get to later chapters. That's not how narrative works. It's common to have a bit of a slow start, or less engaging things at the beginning, but there's a very careful limit to how much of that there can be. If it takes several hours to get to the first actually interesting things, you need to re-structure your story and cut the time wasting exposition."
We can't afford more than 2 hours to know if the game is going to do it or not on Steam: after that point, there is no refuind. I played 2.8 h and I regret it.

Since other think might compensate for this huge problem...maybe it'd be fair to give a neutral evaluations, but since that's not possible, and I feel very frustrated with the agme game for not being able to enjoy it. 

All they'd have to to do was to pu the dialogus in the bubles that on't block movement, or add map events that we woudl ahve to trigger to enter a long blockign dialog so that we'd only start it when we're ready...

Only positive points :
Sory,

erratic gameplay in a very slow paced way - except when you suddenly have a minigame an are on a timer.

[h1]Path of Achra: Charming, Musty... Management Game?[/h1]

[b]TL;DR: Try Path of Achra![/b] But expect to refund it unless you [b]LOVE[/b] autobattler style games.

Booting up Path of Achra I was assaulted with presentation worthy of a cult classic game on DOS. Beautiful and strange music. Weird, ugly, but simultaneously gorgeous hand pixeled visuals. Odd pieces of writing with words and descriptions worthy of a classic fantasy book. And all of this coming together to create a fully alien and primal fantasty world full of new creatures and cultures... [b]I was hooked![/b]

[b]But after 45 minutes of holding right click and watching the game literally play itself,[/b] I had to quit. It wasn't because I got bored, or because I hadn't been entertained. It was something worse than that: Path of Achra had made me feel [i]nothing at all.[/i]

Don't get me wrong: [b]I understand why "Pinball" style games like Path of Achra exist.[/b] You slap together a build hoping for a big synergy, choose a prudent path, then send your little pinball of a character whizzing down the Path, knocking them left and right to stabilize the run with what little inputs you can still give. And I understand why you might enjoy that! And [b]especially[/b] when you get a grip on the game's mad alchemy, and can improvise a [b]truly[/b] explosive concoction to hopefully make it all the way to New Game+ and beyond!

But [b]when the gameplay has been streamlined down to holding right click and watching the game play itself[/b] I immediately start to wonder why the tiles you choose between don't just [b]instantly calculate your run[/b] then summarize the results for you, getting down to the real number crunching management gameplay, that [i]is[/i] the actual meat and potatoes [b]choice making[/b] of the game.

I will admit: Once I got close to the end I did feel a desire to beat Path of Achra, at least just once. But after recently playing Slay the Spire too much I've also realized I no longer want to play games just because "it would be cool to beat them": I want to play games [b]because I like playing them.[/b]

In conclusion Path of Achra has a wealth of flavor and a world I [b]loved[/b] sinking my teeth into, but once that first bite was taken I found I didn't need any more. I recognize why Path of Achra exists and why some people like it, I respect it, and I found it worth trying just for it's uniqueness. But [b]I just wasn't having fun with it.[/b] I've refunded the game glad to have tried it, definetly taking the strange and musty world of Achra with me, as inhospitable and terrible as it is fascinating... if only for an hour or so.

This is one of those games where I genuinely wish Steam had a neutral review option. As much as it hurts me to leave a negative review for a game with such a great art style, all it really offered me beyond that was an interesting setting and world, and a few very well designed characters like our protagonist and the villain, that’s it.

I wish the game had leaned more toward expanding on Michelle’s curse, her conflict with the Duchess, the Duchess’s background, and the exploration of its surreal, demonic world. Instead, constant interruptions from day and night cycles, visually well designed but shallow side characters with their Wattpad like, uninteresting yapfest of side stories seriously hurt the pacing and overall experience, turning it into something closer to a cheap visual novel. 

On top of that, the combat is sometimes even more unbearable than the yapping itself. The game has neither real survival nor horror, just a few elements borrowed from old school survival horror titles, like fixed camera angles and inventory management.

There's a lot to like about this game. The music especially is a highlight - it's clear the devs put a lot of thought into integrating it into every portion of the game, from the environmental sounds, to the attacks, to the rhythm sections. However. the balance on the encounters is off. It's not immediately obvious, but by the time you hit the midgame, fights are a foregone conclusion and kind of a slog. It's also incredibly difficult to avoid fights, and there's no flee mechanic if you get stuck in one. I should not be bored while playing a video game, but I often was, as i mindlessly clicked through the fifth encounter this area. 
       Also, the narrative and writing kind of suck. It gets you from one area/boss encounter to the next, and not much else. The concept of a music driven world, where the battle of the bands is made up of literal battles, is so cool and could go so far in the hands of a decent writer, but unfortunately they didn't have a decent writer.
       I think this is a really cool experiment, and I hope it inspires other devs to explore music-based combat and worlds. But actually playing it isn't fun for the majority of the runtime, so I cannot recomend it.

Disclaimer: I'm more skewed towards a positive review, but I'm so against overwhelming positivity and hype around the game in the indie community that I'm skipping in-between positions and going straight to being a hater here.

The baseline of JRPG+deckbuilder+rhythm game is interesting, but that's mostly it. In the implementation we have a game that's agonizingly stretched between devs' ambitions and oversimplicity. On the ambitions side, we have an idea of an adventure game in general, with needs for different biomes, enemies, a big storyline, NPCs and so on. On top of that, we have a desire to drop on players huge weapons/combat mechanics diversity. But then we are facing the fact that the entire game can be leisurely 100%-ed in 8 hours and just finished in maybe five. 

And here it all starts to crumble into the aforementioned oversimplicity. The plot is incredibely simple (which isn't inherently bad by itself, but it still is probably the most straightforward plot I've seen in years). Characters can as well be non-existent, because they are just functional devices at best or placeholders to fill the map at worst. Puzzles are there, as it feels, just to tick a «Make puzzles» checkbox in the roadmap. The stealth segment ([i]stealth segment[/i], for Gabe's sake, can we just move on to the future from them?) is dull and far-fetched. Yes, the oversimplicity has its own advantages, like non-restrictive fast travel and inventory management or a pretty simple economy, but still, why, if you're making a small rhythm adventure, do you feel the needs to pull up the mechanics checklist for FF or Zelda? Especially when you can't even write [i]two[/i] quotes for NPCs instead of [i]one[/i], which feels really dumb when they are talking about the boss you've already defeated or the problem you've already solved.

And then there's combat mechanics, which goes to the entire opposite side of the spectrum. With roguelike-ish deck/weapon building, devs clearly wanted to give us options for creativity, but 6-hour adventure is NOT a roguelike. And it is not a 20-hour open-world adventure either, where you can feel the urge to experiment naturally. If you need to implement an enemy that punishes the starting build just to force players to change it — it's a bad sign (for the person who decided that Bell Snail is a good enemy design, I have only one question: Why?). Even with my severe case of FOMO, I did only three builds during the playthrough. And since it's an absolutely linear game, its replayability is close to zero, so I will never try all the other ~80% options. It goes to the same «ambitions» category, but it's so glaring that I could not leave it just as a part of it. Also some of the mechanics are way overloaded, but let's move on, or I will get stuck in this section forever.

And then to, probably, the most controversial topic. The music. Guys, nice riffs are NOT good music. And riffs are darn nice, I wouldn't argue with that. But can you tell me why I still remember the opening theme of, say, effin' Balatro, but an hour after closing Fretless I can't even remember if there was one? Yes, I have two looped riffs from the last build stuck in my head, but that's not the same as good music. The game sounds nice, I will give credits where credits are due, but music? Just no. And I understand that in rhythm games music should be utilitarian, but I don't want to go down that rabbit hole of «Ifs, ands, and buts». 

And lastly to the overall game state. It's just not very good up to today's standards. The graphics in the exploration phase are not very readable, with almost no borders or anything that would distinguish objects from one another, especially objects from different vertical layers. The hitboxes are odd when you can be stuck on top of a column while it's actually way below you, but some objects like crates just don't have hitboxes. And other very similar ones have. And I wouldn't even start to talk about the objects that, by every design guideline, should be interactable based on their visuals, but they are just not. In combat most of the interactions are on the downbeat which is very logical. But some are on the upbeat which is still pretty okay. But some feel like they are just not on any beat whatsoever. And let's disregard enemy attacks, because there are, like, 10 enemy types besides bosses, you can trial and error them. But with all the riffs you could at least give us some training dummies to test your abilities on (and if there are any, then good job one more time, because I didn't find them). And the list can go on and on, which is, honestly, a little too much for, once again, a 6-hour game.

Should you still try it? Probably yes. On big discount, almost definitely yes. It's still a pretty charming game with unique mechanic fusion. But let's be clear: this game should be triple or quadruple in length with ten times the team and ten times the budget for all of its parts to really shine. If sales and reviews will give the green light to a big sequel, I'm almost definitely buying it. But this game is a big contender for my personal «Disappointment of the 2025».

There's a lot to like about this game. The music especially is a highlight - it's clear the devs put a lot of thought into integrating it into every portion of the game, from the environmental sounds, to the attacks, to the rhythm sections. However. the balance on the encounters is off. It's not immediately obvious, but by the time you hit the midgame, fights are a foregone conclusion and kind of a slog. It's also incredibly difficult to avoid fights, and there's no flee mechanic if you get stuck in one. I should not be bored while playing a video game, but I often was, as i mindlessly clicked through the fifth encounter this area. 
       Also, the narrative and writing kind of suck. It gets you from one area/boss encounter to the next, and not much else. The concept of a music driven world, where the battle of the bands is made up of literal battles, is so cool and could go so far in the hands of a decent writer, but unfortunately they didn't have a decent writer.
       I think this is a really cool experiment, and I hope it inspires other devs to explore music-based combat and worlds. But actually playing it isn't fun for the majority of the runtime, so I cannot recomend it.

Disclaimer: I'm more skewed towards a positive review, but I'm so against overwhelming positivity and hype around the game in the indie community that I'm skipping in-between positions and going straight to being a hater here.

The baseline of JRPG+deckbuilder+rhythm game is interesting, but that's mostly it. In the implementation we have a game that's agonizingly stretched between devs' ambitions and oversimplicity. On the ambitions side, we have an idea of an adventure game in general, with needs for different biomes, enemies, a big storyline, NPCs and so on. On top of that, we have a desire to drop on players huge weapons/combat mechanics diversity. But then we are facing the fact that the entire game can be leisurely 100%-ed in 8 hours and just finished in maybe five. 

And here it all starts to crumble into the aforementioned oversimplicity. The plot is incredibely simple (which isn't inherently bad by itself, but it still is probably the most straightforward plot I've seen in years). Characters can as well be non-existent, because they are just functional devices at best or placeholders to fill the map at worst. Puzzles are there, as it feels, just to tick a «Make puzzles» checkbox in the roadmap. The stealth segment ([i]stealth segment[/i], for Gabe's sake, can we just move on to the future from them?) is dull and far-fetched. Yes, the oversimplicity has its own advantages, like non-restrictive fast travel and inventory management or a pretty simple economy, but still, why, if you're making a small rhythm adventure, do you feel the needs to pull up the mechanics checklist for FF or Zelda? Especially when you can't even write [i]two[/i] quotes for NPCs instead of [i]one[/i], which feels really dumb when they are talking about the boss you've already defeated or the problem you've already solved.

And then there's combat mechanics, which goes to the entire opposite side of the spectrum. With roguelike-ish deck/weapon building, devs clearly wanted to give us options for creativity, but 6-hour adventure is NOT a roguelike. And it is not a 20-hour open-world adventure either, where you can feel the urge to experiment naturally. If you need to implement an enemy that punishes the starting build just to force players to change it — it's a bad sign (for the person who decided that Bell Snail is a good enemy design, I have only one question: Why?). Even with my severe case of FOMO, I did only three builds during the playthrough. And since it's an absolutely linear game, its replayability is close to zero, so I will never try all the other ~80% options. It goes to the same «ambitions» category, but it's so glaring that I could not leave it just as a part of it. Also some of the mechanics are way overloaded, but let's move on, or I will get stuck in this section forever.

And then to, probably, the most controversial topic. The music. Guys, nice riffs are NOT good music. And riffs are darn nice, I wouldn't argue with that. But can you tell me why I still remember the opening theme of, say, effin' Balatro, but an hour after closing Fretless I can't even remember if there was one? Yes, I have two looped riffs from the last build stuck in my head, but that's not the same as good music. The game sounds nice, I will give credits where credits are due, but music? Just no. And I understand that in rhythm games music should be utilitarian, but I don't want to go down that rabbit hole of «Ifs, ands, and buts». 

And lastly to the overall game state. It's just not very good up to today's standards. The graphics in the exploration phase are not very readable, with almost no borders or anything that would distinguish objects from one another, especially objects from different vertical layers. The hitboxes are odd when you can be stuck on top of a column while it's actually way below you, but some objects like crates just don't have hitboxes. And other very similar ones have. And I wouldn't even start to talk about the objects that, by every design guideline, should be interactable based on their visuals, but they are just not. In combat most of the interactions are on the downbeat which is very logical. But some are on the upbeat which is still pretty okay. But some feel like they are just not on any beat whatsoever. And let's disregard enemy attacks, because there are, like, 10 enemy types besides bosses, you can trial and error them. But with all the riffs you could at least give us some training dummies to test your abilities on (and if there are any, then good job one more time, because I didn't find them). And the list can go on and on, which is, honestly, a little too much for, once again, a 6-hour game.

Should you still try it? Probably yes. On big discount, almost definitely yes. It's still a pretty charming game with unique mechanic fusion. But let's be clear: this game should be triple or quadruple in length with ten times the team and ten times the budget for all of its parts to really shine. If sales and reviews will give the green light to a big sequel, I'm almost definitely buying it. But this game is a big contender for my personal «Disappointment of the 2025».

Bit meh if I'm honest but an easy play through and achievement FC. I liked the danger within the level progression but all of a sudden that stopped and was replaced with harmless fog? The enemies after this stage are easier to dodge than the earlier levels, including a final 'boss' level that has 300 prestige per run. There's a lack of difficulty curve to finish the game it's pretty much handed to you. The game does have a good price point and kept me engaged for the playthrough but it isn't a game I would rush to recommend to anyone.

Frustating and boring. The prestige system is so bad. 
The game starts way too hard for a "relaxing" game. Major quality of lifes are locked behind way too expensive upgrades in comparison to pure stats buffs, which makes the game even more depthless and repetitive!
Had to ask for a refund... 
I really wanted to like this game, it has everything i like from thematics to core gameplay, but it need some tweaks:
・Make it easier in the beginning; remove the timer mechanic or rework the years thing 
・Rework the hazards for a more interesting approach
・Add another currency that are tangent to the quality of life upgrades i mentioned. 
・Add another modes
was hoping for a chill game, more frustrating than anything

This is a complicated one to review. I really like the developer(s) and they went above and beyond to make this an engaging game but I'm sad to say it's not for me. I like the concepts of 4D and how it interacts with other dimensions but as a golf game, it became all too common to just switch back and forth between 3D and 4D view (vertical is switched with the fourth dimension) or holding the "align with hole" button.

+ Lots of courses with pretty some cool designs.
+ The 4D art in the museum level is especially cool.
+ Cute story with very cool surprise at the end.

- The music is, I'm sorry to say, really grating after not too long. The dynamic nature of it is cool, though.
- Unfortunately it's "just a golf game", really. - It may add a dimension, but not more gameplay (other than a marble run mode).

It's not for me and if you don't enjoy golf games, it's not really for you either. It's a technical achievement and plays very smooth.

I am happy to support the creator, the game is well made and will not refund, but also will not recommend.

i love this game, 4d games are very rare and it is impressive to see them. but the grid on the third course gives me an unplayable amount of motion sickness and im not seeing any way to turn it off. the default settings are also bad, you're gunna want to change them. if you dont have issues with motion sickness, i would highly recommend

Unfortunately as was my experience with Hyperbolica, this is a really cool engine with some awesome concepts... but not a very good game. There's no glut of vapid NPCs this time thank goodness, but that doesn't stop the "plot" from happening at you when a higher-dimensional being with a one-dimensional sense of humor decides to show up between worlds.

I really want this dev to keep making engines, but the projects themselves need to be handed off to writers. And designers.

This game is a really neat exploration of the 4th dimension - and a really bad mini golf game. The game absolutely skyrockets in difficulty after the first couple of courses. The golf physics feel so strange - you bounce WAY too hard off of surfaces. It makes the game feel unpredictable and incredibly difficult to use. I can't recommend this game to anyone looking for a good mini golf game.

Working in more than three dimensions is a lot of fun, actually. I managed to wrap my head around it faster than I had expected, which is a credit to the excellent design of that system. So, A+ on the "4D" part.

That leaves the "Golf" part, which you'd think would be easier to get right, but apparently not. There's a weird obsession here with hills that require pinpoint accuracy in the power of your swing, multiple times per hole, and it's not helped by reinventing the swinging power mechanic. I wouldn't count myself that great of a mini-golf player, but if I can hit par on more holes than not, something's wrong if I'm hitting +98 on others.

Get this on sale if you can. The fun of learning to work in higher dimensions is dragged down by poor course design.

I love the concept, and the first couple of courses are fine enough, but by the third (Arctic) course it just became immensely frustrating and I stopped having fun. You're welcome to try it, and maybe I'll come back to it some other time, but I don't think I'll be finishing this one for now.
Please add controller support. And please do more play testing. The basic controls are not what I expect from a minigolf game, even in 4D.

first of all, the 5d golf with the beyond course isnt supported by the level editor or the steam workshop which albeit not the main mode of the game was one of the big elements i was interested in seeing from the devlog and made me pick it up to see how it handled it since i havent really tried a game like that and i couldnt really grasp it with videos, but just ends up being a gimmick for a fraction of the game 
also, despite the trippy epileptic visuals in the beyond mode there is no way to dampen or disable the visuals (despite the 5d tutorial actually being a grey blocky design before switching to the trippy colors) and not being able to make courses in the workshop with 5d means that its just locked behind a visual theme thats actively making understanding how 5d works worse

now, for the 4d content, its Alright but barebones and what it tries to make up for from the simple geometry it fails in course design just ends up either feeling irritating to navigate or simple to the point its just 3d golf with walls that sometimes grow closer to the hole that you can tell where they are by moving the 4d camera
if you enable the volume mode its just 3d golf with antigravity which albeit easier to play isnt really 4d golf at all outside of being a projection of the normal level that is 4d (the only reason i ever didnt enable it was either to at least enjoy the gimmick or because it cant handle ramps alongside wanting to move on to the 5d segment)

as stated in other reviews the golf physics itself is really weird, and a highlight of this problem with my experience was with the 7th hole in the nebula course where the extent of the hole is angling your 4d axis so the entire course is visible in your slice and hitting your ball onto a slanted antigravity wall which usually leads to you sliding off, making the challenge less about navigating 4d but just reaching another platform

the 20 dollar price tag for a gimmick that the game encourages you to actively avoid is shocking, especially if you consider the content thats actually in the game lacks much replay value unless youre achievement hunting for getting the best scores and the level editor and workshop doesnt have a lot of tools to work with to make anything distinct let alone a userbase thats actively making it

codeparade makes good content but i wouldnt be so quick to judge the positive reviews as anything more than fans who went out of their way to pick it up instead of people reviewing off of the actual substance of the game

Couldn't play for more than 10 minutes. Tried very hard to actually enjoy this but it was genuinely unplayable. Controls are also awful.

Edit: I should probably elaborate. I wanted to get this game because I thought that it would be really cool to "experience" the fourth dimension in this way. When i got this, the controls felt very annoying and hard to use and I really just did not enjoy it. I do not recommend this, as it feels very low quality and thrown together.

Man I really want to like this game, but it's so damn hard to figure out where you're supposed to go.  I'll walk through the course before I putt and think I know where I'm going, but as soon as I start putting, I lose track of the path.  And there's very little to orient yourself with, it's all just checkerboard textures as far as the eye can see.  Like a real-life golf course would have little statues of colored golf balls decorating the place or something.
I really wish controller support was better, will change recommendation if it improves
Couldn't beat the tutorial, way too complicated to wrap my head around. There are better golf games out there that don't screw with your head.
There is too little 4D content, so it is essentially a three-dimensional pinball game.

This game is adorable and relaxing I do want to start off with that first. If you're looking for a super short I-Spy game this is it, plenty of achievements as well, though that's not something I care about when it comes to any kind of games whether it's mobile games, PC or console games. 

Let me start off by listing the pros.

The music is relaxing and it is quite nice to just sit and relax and try to spot the cats throughout the entire black and white map. The overall artwork is unique and lovely as well. Even the "weirdly shaped" cats are adorable. The whole image that you have to search through is cool with a lot of small still scenes happening as well as some small, super cool details. Overall, I think the game is good but could be better. The game is also pretty cheap so if you're looking for a cheap game that's satisfying for the first little while, this is it. 

Cons:

Once you finish the basic black and white map, the only thing you unlock is the same exact map, but in color. Nothing changes except for the color. The cats are still all in the same exact places. There are no new maps you unlock by completing the first one. If you plan on beating it then maybe returning to it in the future so that maybe by that time you have forgotten where all of the cats are, then sure, it works. But I will not be buying anymore games from this developer as it seems most of their games have the same concept. Buy the cheap game, beat the first level in black and white then unlock the same map but in color with nothing else changed. 

Again it is a cute game but it really sucks that there wasn't more to it. By no means do I think this game is bad, I just think it's disappointing how little there is to it. I have played mobile games just like this where there is so much more things to do, all for free and you only have to pay $5 to get rid of ads. 

I would personally recommend games like that on mobile. They are just as good and even if you have to watch ads, there are so many more levels you can do. Hundreds that you can unlock for $5 compared to one game like this for a buck or two that only gives you one level (and that same level in color once you beat it)

game_name: Star Fetchers : Escape from Pork Belly
review_text:
Released a year ago and it's still this bad? Honestly seems unfinished. Also insane pricing for a 30 minute experience.

Unskippable cutscenes and a TERRIBLE ost... Really sad about the music in this dlc because that was the main reason I became a fan of the star fetchers pilot. Tiny 5 second songs with very obvious loop points in awkwardly long unskippable cutscenes which you've seen again and again because you can't save your progress.

Gameplay is very simple and is honestly ruined by the garbo OST. You punch, dodge, and earn money for powerups as you win matches. For some reason theres phonk with samples from counter-strike harrasing my ears though,

Honestly if this dlc was free, I still wouldn't waste my time with it :D

It's fun for what it is, but there is a not insignificant number of randomly generated puzzles that are unsolvable. You can still win by skipping them, but it's unsatisfying for me personally.

The premise is interesting, but the execution of this visual novel falls flat. The real time sync is an interesting idea, but the waiting periods are too varied in length and generally too long to add value to the experience. They can fortunately be skipped with left shift in the main game and right alt in the postgame. My second biggest gripe with the game is the writing and English translation - the writing quality varies from great to poor and often the canned responses don't give you any choice whatsoever. For instance, when Aliya learns you have a heartbeat monitor and asks you to turn it off, you can pick from three variants of "okay" as responses, meanwhile you can leave the monitor on for the rest of the game with no further reference to it. The worldbuilding is moderately interesting, but too few characters and corporations are introduced to really make the universe feel believable and alive. The postgame episode expands on that a bit but feels significantly more linear and fan-fic like compared to the rest of the game's writing.

The art is really cute. The music is fine but not inspiring, and the game unfortunately incentivizes you to leave the radio on at all times and scan frequencies occasionally, but most of the time you'll just be hearing static from it.

Ultimately I felt sympathetic for Aliya but not emotionally invested in her story, possibly due to poor writing or poor translation. If you have more free time and less budget for games, it might be worth a shot if you like the premise, otherwise I can't really recommend it. Try something like OPUS: Echo of Starsong which has a very similar vibe but is put together much better.

Feels like its your just reading a transcript of AI chat-bot RP-ing than an actual 'game' designed by a creative. Pretty uninteresting prompt used to generate this game, imo, if this was really just a grab from chat bot history. Wouldn't even recommend this if it was free. Game is super slow as they fill in wait time to simulate actions and text/speech time. They really want you to sit and wait while no gameplay is happening.

'Is This Seat Taken?' is a fairly easy logic puzzle game with a simplistic art style and a cute but simple story about fitting in. The game's general design makes it feel like a tablet/phone game, and I would say that it would genuinely fit perfectly on that platform. This overall simplicity of the game can fit to some people, but for me it unfortunately really missed some depth. 

While there is certainly some variety present, levels can start to feel repetitive rather quickly. The game is at it's strongest whenever a new puzzle element gets introduced, but then loses its momentum by repeating an earlier puzzle right after, without adding anything new or really increasing their difficulty. In my eyes the game would've benefited from a deeper exploration of unique ways to keep the player challenged and engaged with the same kind of logic puzzles.
The charm of the game runs out quickly and becomes boring.

Not a bad game but not great either. Once you learn to clump together groups that can have conflicts with others (like people who smell bad) you've solved a sizable number of the puzzles in the game. And it won't take long to come to that conclusion, it's just common sense. Multiple times even late into the game I'd just take a cursory glance at the needs of each character, slot them in by instinct, and then I'd only be 1 or 2 swaps off of solving the level. I have no idea how they could fix that in this kind of game, adding too many layers of interaction would certainly make the game harder, but it would also just be insanely annoying. 

There is a "story" that tries to break up the monotony, but it isn't a very good one so I guess I should be glad it doesn't take up much time. 

Presentation is nice but the fairly flat difficulty curve and lack of variety makes this feel like one of those daily puzzle games a lot of websites are putting out. The game probably would have been better in small doses, but I didn't feel like buying a game just to ration it out in 15 minute chunks over the course of weeks.

fun but needs more content, only takes a few hours to 100% complete
lacks variety in level type and requisites, I would've liked to have seen more creative ones like the special levels
the plot is really boring - while it's sparse and pretty quick to click through, it desperately needs improvement (or a skip button that doesn't require game completion to use)
not worth it at full price, I'd only purchase on sale
This isn't a BAD game, but it lacks substance. It doesn't really justify the price tag.

Is This Seat Taken is a fine game, but I struggle thinking who I would recommend it to. 

It's an LSAT seating logic puzzle game in visual format. Characters have different traits and wants (Smells bad and wants a window seat) and you need to place them in ever shrinking spaces with other characters whose desires conflict with them. 

Oh and while this is happening, you follow the story of a rhombus who wants to break into acting... but this world of shapes is racist and the only other Rhombus in the business is a jerk. To be honest, these story beats feel inconsequential and I didn't care about them at all. 

The art is fine, the puzzles are okay (though the nature of them often feels limited), and I actively dislike some of the framing. For example there's a train level where you need to load both people and cargo but the cargo is basically a nothingburger. Theres a section in a movie theater where people wearing hats cant have anyone sitting behind them because they block view... and then they bring this back with standing room for stadium seating. Or like... there's several scenarios with a concert and you have to place the band on stage and there's no where else they can go. 

Is This Seat Taken just made me feel kind of bored. And at full price of $10 for 3-4 hours of gameplay I just don't see the value.

I like the premise of the game and it has a charming design, but as a puzzle game it falls terribly short.

The game took under 4 hours to clear, and there were only a couple of times I had to stop and think for a moment about the arrangement of the characters within that time. Most solutions are about simple reading comprehension as characters will either be the type to tell you where they explicitly want to be (left side, back row, exactly next to another), or they'll have such loose requirements that many positions will suffice. Combine this with the fact that sometimes you'll have a group that demands to be together, and limited places for that to happen, and most things just slot into place with just a tertiary glance. There is very little difficulty to be found within.

I also have complaint that levels are split into multiple stages, but the new elements in each stage very rarely seem to require you to move around what was previously placed to reach the new solution. Why is there little to no mix up? Why is there no increase to the difficulty or complexity of the solutions as you advance? 

Finally there's systems that mark your completion of the levels, with stars (awarded per character you complete the requests of), and getting a 'perfect' (for completing all requests) which serve as a sort of pat on the head for playing the game. If you were to not engage with actually fulfilling the demands of ALL the characters, would there even be a game to be had? There's a huge difference from 'partially solved' to 'solved' when it comes to puzzles!

Unless you're new to puzzles and don't mind a lot of repetition, then give this game a pass.

It's a puzzle game but not the kind with unique solutions, and I guess that's on me for not expecting something so casual. I just never felt like I needed to be very smart to solve any of the puzzles so I can't recommend it as a [i]puzzle[/i] game.

nothing against this game nor the devs, but after playing the first of five missions in 13 minutes, nothing really was appealing to me. very cute artstyle and colour palette, but beyond that, it felt like a fairly... empty? game (not to discredit the work put into it ofc!!). there wasn't anything that especially stood out to me. i can see what the devs were going for, but i think it would vastly improve with more customisability and variations in gameplay - it feels like once you've figured out something, then you can't really improve on that. it's just... done.

it might just be me tbf, but i feel like this is more of a barebones mobile game rather than a cosy game i could really enjoy. if it gets better after the first mission then i'm sorry: but, for me at least, i didn't see a reason to keep playing. while i get that a simple level is important for a tutorial, it also needs to hook the player, and personally, it didn't hook me. after the first few minutes, i was already wondering if it was worth my time, and once those 13 minutes passed, i knew that it definitely wasn't my kind of cosy game. it's probably due to personal preference, and not necessarily the game's fault, but that's still something worth talking about in a review in case someone else might feel the same way :)

i usually wouldn't leave a negative review on an indie creation, and the only reason i *am* is because the game has overwhelmingly positive reviews so it won't dent it too much - i just want people who are on the fence to have a wider range of opinions to look at lol! i won't refund this game (not just bc i bought it in a bundle, but also bc indie devs absolutely deserve support, even if i personally didn't gel with their game), and no hate to the devs in the slightest! good luck with any future endeavours!! i'm glad others could enjoy this game, even if i didn't! /gen :333

Meh, it has potential. It's cute but childish. Easy to learn, easy to master. Only 5 levels. It  I has the potential of becoming addictive but by the end of the 3rd round I wasn't overly caring. Its REALLY easy to figure out what people will want and clicking on the side stuff... is just clicking on side stuff. Not clicking on side stuff just means you get a couple fewer dollars. By the 5th level the street got so long & cumbersome I stopped half hearting it. I'd hit start the day, speed 3, then get up & get a snack or go to the bathroom. For $5 on sale fine it entertained me for a couple hours but then I realized this was it. The developers only half assed their effort. There isn't any more to it. So ya if it's on sale for super cheap & you need something to kill a couple hours go for it. But seriously it's so easy to 100% this in just a couple hours it's not worth more. I have no idea how this has so many good reviews at this time unless they're all moms rating it for their preschoolers.

I am well aware this is an early access game, but personally I didn't see anything special about the game.  Adding the elements to the Tetris blocks was a cool mechanic, but the actual placement of the blocks seemed arbitrary and didn't really add to the gameplay for me.  I think it's super cool that it's a roguelite/roguelike, this was the sole reason for my purchase..  The monsters you are killing off had decent animations, but the tower design, the maze and the overall aesthetic, were very lackluster.  Maybe I will check it out again, but there are plenty of tower defense games out there that are much better, fully developed and much cheaper or free to play.  Or maybe I just have early access fatigue lol.  I really wish steam would add a neutral option but at this point the game is not worth $15 bucks.

An interesting take on tower defense that borders on fun.  Unfortunately, any time a tower feels strong, the developer hamfistedly nerfs it.  Hell, one tower that was fun to use got limited to 1 a map!  Let players have fun in single player games!
The game takes a lot of time for how not hard it is. Feels more like an attention test than anything satisfying.

Definetlely fun to look at as a fan of the Moomins - it's really cute - but gameplay wise it's pretty underwhelming. I'd say this would only be enjoyable for people who never play video games or children, due to it's simplicity. The dialog is also pretty generic. Kind of dissapointing. Let's see if the next Moomin game has more to offer.

There's a bug near the end that you can't fix so can't complete the game. Lots of people have had this issue and the devs still aren't fixing it. Also very hard to do things like run/jump sometimes as the game glitches.

This game is SUPER CUTE but ultimately frustrating for a player like me. This doesn't mean the game is bad, but for you puzzle gamers out there who hate timed puzzles, don't play this one. If you have good response skills and hand eye coordination, you will like this game! For the rest of us, prepare to cry.

Thankfully it doesn't punish you too badly, just resetting you back a few steps so you can try again, and you don't lose items or progress. But after failing to hide from the police officers time and time again, I just couldn't do it anymore. I'm miserable at timed puzzles. Like, it's pathetic! I know it's a me problem. I wish games like this had an accessibility option to make the police slower or to increase my sprint time (it's very limited). 

RIP cute game, maybe my kids with awesome reflexes will be able to play you.

I got this at half the price, and while I can't recommend the game at half or full price, let me start with the positives. 

I appreciate the gorgeous artwork and the music - Sigur Ros! beautiful music! The Moomins - wonderful! I had lovely memories of both and thought this might be a good game to get my niece.

Ok now the negatives
The game play is too short and way too linear for its price point. And though it was short - the whole gameplay felt like a slog - there was never any feeling of discovery or wonder.  It's a game. It's should be a good game first, and then you can add the frills. If you're doing a cozy quest/adventure game, there should be secrets, there should be collectables, maybe craftables, and more dialogue. This was a rip-off at sale price. I should have requested a refund before an hour into the game, but I was in a bit of disbelief about how bad the gameplay was and kept telling myself - this is just the tutorial, this is just the first act. Surely they game devs will stop railroading the story by the second act... oh it's almost finished... that was it?

The sheer lack of user friendliness is offensive - it's not a visual novel, it's a game. Why did we have an inventory? I opened it once. You would have had to have been blind to have missed the pages of the play. And apart from that the only other non-linear quest I can remember were the muskrat's (who doesn't appear on the map) and that's pretty much it... all the other questlines tied into the main story. I can understand that it's aimed at a younger audience but I couldn't recommend it to them either. There's no joy in the gameplay. It's simply many fetch quests and a few stealth sneaks and chases. And why is there an upper cap to the inspiration? Surely there is a better way of both rewarding exploration and diligence?

Overall a 4/10 for me

TLDR; This game was actually pretty good, UNTIL I decided to do the achievements, then it showed its true colors.  I probably will come back to this and get the last few achievements (because I'm stubborn and apparently hate myself).  But, that won't take long and honestly the damage has already been done, my opinion isn't changing.

You have to unlock both sprint AND CROUCH.  Core movement mechanics that you should just have from the start...  I can think of several different upgrades to allow INSTEAD of those, which would mitigate many of my complaints.  A simple one would be the painfully awful amount of time it takes to tie bags, but you're stuck with SLOW for the entire game.  You could also implement a "leaf highlight" system, since that's not in the game at all for some reason...

Visual filters reset every game reload, not a big deal, but also incredibly easy to solve and avoids forcing people to switch it EVERY TIME THEY START YOUR GAME.

The worst achievement in the game glitched, forcing me to do it twice.  I was doing the achievement for finishing the game without unlocking a single vent, I had a pile nearly the size of the trash can when some leaves started floating upwards.  BIG SURPRISE, that room had ~70 leaves that no longer existed anywhere.  I had to do the entire challenge over again.  In total this wasted around 2.5-3 hours, and 90% of that time was spent STANDING IN ONE PLACE holding left click, then right click, OVER AND OVER.  "FUN".

No keybinds menu at all, just a legend.  Then to make it even worse, any connected controller disables keyboard input when detected.  This includes a set of joytsticks that I normally just leave plugged in for flying games.

SUMMARY: Worst $6 I've spent in at least a year.  If you don't care about completing the goals that games provides for you, then it's wonderful.  But if you care at all about actually finishing games, this feels like the dev is actively trying to waste your time.
Fun while it lasted, which was about an hour. Super short game.

It's a good thing it was extremely cheap. That was so short, I thought I must have missed something in the main gameplay. Nope, just a few achievements that don't add any real value. The premise is sound, but not worth spending money on for the amount of gameplay. You can achieve the same with free apps. If new content or maps are added, I might change my review, but not if it is a paid add-on, it isn't worth it.

My first ever review, and my first ever refund request.

The game is calm and compelling. I enjoyed the hour I spent on it. However, progress was soft-locked due to a bug. A missing leaf on the basket ball court area, which prevented unlocking the sprint skill, which prevented progress through the garage and into the rest of the game.

Searching for solutions, it seems these "missing leaf" issues and soft-locking progression is reasonably common. The solution - send your save file to the developer. This is generally unacceptable, and certainly unacceptable from a system security point of view.

Others reported restarting the game would have the missing leaf reappear. Tried this several times, with no success.

If the developer adds a way to fix this issue in-game (auto-complete an area at 99.9%, say), I would repurchase.

Definitely closer to 'Digging a Hole' than Powerwash imo. Short, simple, wysiwyg. If that's your thing, go for it, but maybe only on sale.

I personally found the upgrades almost unneeded- they sped the game up so quickly that it felt cheap to use them, and the ending was... out of left field (again, like Digging a Hole.) I'd probably go back in if there was more content, but I wasn't super impressed.

Kinda meh, to be honest. Could've been alright, but it's just too full of filler content. The art style is alright. The story is ok. Some of the characters and interactions are pretty cool and cute. Combat is kinda basic, but I wasn't expecting that much. But then the game just goes on and on, throwing copy/pasted room assets with copy/pasted anemies at you. It's huge, aimless and a real drag to explore. You can buy a hint on where to go, but it's a single use expenditure, and a pricy one. The map is not very clear. You can't mark it or make notes, though at least you can buy location tips for all the items you may have missed. Lots and lots of backtracking all over the place to see if you can finally go somewhere. A two or three hour game that bloated into ten.

An interesting game, maybe a nice intro to metroidvanias, but for me it just did not hit. I felt as if the combat was pretty repetitive, the gameplay areas were either too simple or too obscure, and the narrative and ending didn't make up for the experience.

I think the concept of being a janitor in a dangerous afterlife was interesting, but I wish that there were more janitor-y game mechanics, such as mopping up spots while monsters chase you, wiping windows, taking out bags of trash, etc. But instead this plays as a very formulaic Zelda type adventure game. The art is cute, but it is not worth the full price.

I played through most of the game, ~85% completion after 15 hours (hard difficulty). Unfortunately, the progression falls flat quite quickly, to a point where it barely feels like a Metroidvania. There is your classical ability gating, but not much more. There are just two types of collectibles that affects gameplay. One is an equippable special attack. From the ~15 that I collected, only a single one felt useful at all (you can only equip one at a time). The other collectibles represent passive buffs. Those were in part interesting and useful. I could buy a single health upgrade right at the start of the game, and that was already the entire HP progression that the game had to offer?! Towards the end, I had a ton of currency, but there's simple nothing useful to buy with it. With progression and exploration being such a dissapointment, I sadly can't recommend this game - at least not as a Metroidvania.
bland, repetitive, lacked inspiration, art work was half decent, functional as a game, just not fun....3/10

game_name: Duck Detective: The Ghost of Glamping
review_text: I'll have to say "nah" but it's a very personal "nah". The humor doesn't land and the game relies on it landing so... nah.

game_name: Duck Detective: The Ghost of Glamping
review_text:
Despite being charming, witty and having the most adorable main character of any game, its a furry Benoit Blanc, the second installment again suffers from the same issues as the first. 

Pros+
+ If anything is carrying the game it is its lovable yet flawed bread addicted protagonist, oozing with charm yet hopeless
+ The supporting cast of the game also stands out on their own, though the short game means character development suffers.
+ The gameplay is a good detective model but again some elements are superfluous and doesn't add anything to the game, its a stripped down Golden Idol mechanic as it is.
+ Love the graphics and cutout style animation.
+ Music is great but tends to become repetitive after a while.
+ short simple, good for a palate cleaner in between more series long gameplays

Cons-
- Too short, game can be completed within 2 hours, it would work better if this was released as a five episodic series rather than a standalone game.
- For the price, wait for a deep sale discount to enjoy it, not worth the full price.
- Some of the puzzles have no real deducktive reasoning and need guess work, the environmental clues and talks doesn't even provide info most times.
- Story is just meh, nothing great.
- More suited as a kids game and intro to point and click detective games for adults
- Puzzles are simple yet still frustrating at times.
- The confusing sudden twist in the late game storyline feels out of place though the absurdity does add to the story in some ways, but way off the bat...
- So much potential but alas....

game_name: Duck Detective: The Ghost of Glamping
review_text: It was ok... worth it if it's on significant sale maybe and you love even mid detective games. The actual mystery solving was pretty underwhelming. The game is quite short, and there are several interesting areas that felt pretty underutilized. Did not feel as robust as the first game, and characters were less engaging. It's mostly carried by the comic dialogue and character design, and that kind of thing is done better in Frog Detective or Tangled Tower.
