This game fell short on several fronts.  Individually they are not game-ruining, but they add up to 'no'.

overall design
[list]
  [*]level design - every level seems to include the masked shaman enemies.  the game needs more enemy variety.
rooms in each level feel way too small. they are almost all rectangles where the 4 corners are entrance, exit, and treasure chests.
  [*]music - the music is not very noticeable, even after turning it up to max in the settings. when you choose which level to go to next, out of 2 choices, every pair of levels seems to share the same music.  1 pair has some more noticeable music, but i think it's an 8 second loop?
  [*]arbitrary limit - there is a cap of how many relics you can have, which is too easy to hit. so the choice between "power-ups" vs relics for rewards is not as much of a choice as it seems.  is surprising for this type of game too which usually about amassing as many upgrade items as possible (including duplicates, which do not seem to be allowed here)
[/list]
annoyances
[list] 
  [*]coins bounce and fly everywhere.  whether from opening a chest or just walking by some enemies you've killed, it's common for the coin to start moving toward you but then overshoot you by a mile and you have to chase it down.  you can land an arm's length from a coin and it will fly halfway across the screen (or yes even offscreen).  picking up coins is harder than the combat, seriously.
  [*]after unlocking the condition for a class, then you have to pay a currency for it, which is the same currency you can get weapon or unlocks for.  early on, a class will cost up to 7 times a weapon/secondary unlock, making it hard to spend it on a class that could be hit or miss and doesn't lead to future unlocks
  [*]there is a relic compendium which is completely filled out before you have even gotten any of them.  i wish it only showed full details for ones you have gotten.
  [*]it's possible for a relic you don't want (had a huge downside) to land in front of the lever of the slot machine making it impossible to keep pulling the lever without picking it up, even if you try to jump and grab the lever from higher. and no, you can't drop a relic unless you pick up more than you can store
  [*]on the game loading screen, rather than show your total playtime on the save file, it shows how much time you have spent on your current run. so if you usually save and exit from the hub, you're going to always see 00:00 on it.  not very helpful.
[/list]
bugs
[list]
  [*]three times when exiting the game, it got stuck on a loading screen and i had to kill it from task manager
  [*]a couple times the character got stuck doing the attack animation for at least a dozen seconds after i let go of all the buttons. may have been at the end of battles to release companions
  [*]cat claws weapon keeps displaying that you unlock it every time you pick it up
  [*]fire-breathing salamanders when knocked off a ledge get disconnected from their fire breath which is still magically shooting from their previous location
[/list]

[h2]Unrewarding[/h2]
[*]Game has a tutorial for the bare essentials, but is somehow missing to state how to get which currency (which is relevant for permanent upgrades).
[*]Upgrades are too small & insignificant to feel relevant in any way, shape or form (+1% defense, that reset after you die, yay!).
[*]In the 1.5 hours I played, I didn't come across any story at all.
[*]Dead in 2-4 hits.
[*]Dash has an excessively long cooldown compared to other games.
[*]Every time you die, you still have to walk through that whole upgrade area even though you know you haven't earned enough currency for any permanent upgrades. At times, you may spend more time walking through that area than you've spent on the previous run.
[*]If compared to Astral Ascent, loses in every single aspect

i don't think I have ever wasted more time on trying to get an achievement 10% of people had that is 100% RNG when I was already done every other achievement.
I'm closing in on 100 games with 100% achievements and would love for this to be one of them but just can't be bothered to chase this goofy RNG achievement when I've done everything else long ago. 
It actually has soured any positive thoughts I had about this game. Awful design choice.

I just finished the normal mode of the game and I have some thoughts to share. The game's atmosphere is relaxing and the fights are quite euphoric, but I felt something was missing. Although the combat had new mechanics that grow exponentially throughout the game, such as parrying and healing, the euphoria only comes from dodging attacks. The characters give the illusion of being very diverse, but this can only be seen during battles, as their designs are not only very similar but also too simplistic and mediocre. The plot is confusing; plot twists rain down, and you become as disoriented as Kobako and Toaka, ultimately just fighting whatever appears without much room to question or reflect on them before new plot twists fall upon you. I really liked the scenes where the names of the attacks appear, but I wish they were more dramatic or lasted longer, since the battles are extraordinarily quick if you're at least a decent player. 

A cute game, but it had a lot of potential to be better!
Congrats on the game!

Sadly, yet another promising shoot em up ruined by trying to be half shoot em up, half roguelite slop em up, and not particularly good at either. 

The main problem revolves around the upgrade system - it's an advancing selector system like Gradius, but it misses a couple of bits central to the design that make that game work. Number one, unlike Gradius, you don't get to control where upgrades spawn, only when. They can get pushed under level geometry or enemies with absolutely 0 possibility of getting them back, or the map itself can suddenly switch making them unobtainable. Number two, it has an absolutely punishing power curve, where falling even a couple of upgrade tokens behind is a run-ender. There's no way to push back ahead of the curve and play more skillfully, there's no version of this game where you're alive at all if you don't get more or less all of the speed upgrades very early into a run.

The game itself seems to realise this is a shonky system and gives you multiple upgrade cards to pull glimmer (the xp currency) and upgrade tokens at longer ranges, or even across the map, and the game is reasonably fun with those. Trouble is - you have absolutely no control over when those spawn, either. It's basically mashing a deterministic remember 'em up kind of game like Gradius into a sloppy who cares game like Vampire Survivors, only without the predictability of the former, or the persistent upgrades of the latter. 

In a committed slop em up, the XP/token attractors would be in a permanent upgrades page. Unfortunately this game wants more than anything to be taken seriously as a scoreboard shooter, so it doesn't quite have the courage to go there. 

It does not really work out that well. The urge to make a hiscore shooter completely trips over the urge to make it a roguelite, and it ends up not being very good at either. Your runs get cut short by stuff you could never have controlled, and your high scores are just a list of the runs where you got something that pulled upgrades out from the walls the game plonked over them at random.

If you're looking for something combining bullet hell, boss rush and roguelites that doesn't tragically misunderstand all the genres involved, The Void Rains Upon Her Heart is what you're after instead.

I think it's probably a good game for the right crowd, but to me it looked like the roguelike parts would be a major component and they frankly aren't. How your allocate your upgrades and what cards you choose have next to no impact on how you actually interact with the game at all, leaving me to wonder why they were added in the first place. It doesn't change how you deal with bosses, all of which are pretty much hard coded to be "do X until you win or mess up and die"

It's like the le croix of roguelikes. There's sort of a hint of flavor there, but really it's JUST a top down techno arcade shooter, and it's fully competent at being that but I have been playing those for decades and it's not what I expected from the trailers and reviews. 

Asking me to make meaningless choices is worse than just no choices at all in a game like this where you require a ton of focus and concentration and a misstep just ends your run. If the roguelike bits had any weight to them, or if it just stripped those away (including the vaguely pointless stat upgrades, there's clearly a "best" route for those) and focused on just being more of an arcade experience, I think I'd like it more, but I'm not excited at all for this in between thing.
Dude where’s my I-frames?

It's beautiful on the surface: Thumper meets Geometry Wars. For several hours I felt like I just needed to get smarter. Then I died on the rainbow bridge bonus for the 12th time because the background pink floor matches the soon to disappear red floor, instantly killing runs where i have 4 shields and never been touched. This is a Bonus scoring mode you should want to achieve by collecting tokens at the right time to spell out a word, and half of my runs have ended in this mode. I actively avoid the token at all costs now. That shouldn't be the goal of design. The red on red needs a change. I also don't like that the blaster gets stronger but slower to fire as you upgrade it, meaning you start ramming into enemies that used to be cleared out of the way. Feels like a punishment for upgrading. And the bosses have a bit more varied pattern recognition than I would like. It's the small stuff that's adding up to feeling like a slight miss the more I get into it. Great production values but the gameplay flaws are frustrating.

game_name: Crypt of the NecroDancer: Hatsune Miku Character DLC
review_text: I did not like this one. Hatsune Miku does not fit in the setting here, and I feel like her character is too annoying to play as.

game_name: Crypt of the NecroDancer: Hatsune Miku Character DLC
review_text: I did not like this one. Hatsune Miku does not fit in the setting here, and I feel like her character is too annoying to play as.

I was rather excited for the game based on the reviews and the description. The idea of navigating an overworld to find hidden levels is definitely neat. I was quite charmed by the process of uncovering the first couple. Unfortunately after the first few hours I honestly could not muster the motivation to keep playing.

I can only remember a handful of levels that didn't just feel like one locked room of enemies followed by another. The combat wasn't particularly interesting with the simpler enemies, and just felt rather frustrating when they got more complicated or, more often, crowded. Perhaps there was some extra tool hidden somewhere but as far as I got I didn't have many options to play with beyond trying to walk into enemies. The gun's limited reach and damage didn't help its case, and the fact it ran out of ammo so quickly made it feel like I couldn't rely on it for much beyond getting in some negligible chip damage between actually hitting enemies with my sword.

While searching for secrets is obviously a key part of the concept, it never felt like I was finding anything particularly interesting in the levels. Just a lot of compost and the occasional coin. Sometimes I'd find a food and feel clever but then remember food is just a temporary health buff that I only felt the urge to use when a level was bothersome or, more often, because I was out of space for new food anyway. 

I tried to keep going but once the basics of the gameplay lost their novelty I couldn't find anything else to pull me in. Tets and Arkas's interactions were cute but nothing else about the world caught my attention. Maybe I'm missing some part of this picture but at this point I don't want to keep looking.

Angeline Era immediately caught my attention with its music and visual style, and based on the gameplay videos I watched, I thought I would enjoy playing it. However, the gameplay feels stiff and unfun, and the characters talk too much without saying anything meaningful. The game just didn’t ‘flow’ for me.

I'm a fan of Analgesic but this was a big miss for me. The exploration can be fun, the vibes are so good and the music fits the game very well but basically everything else was a disappointment. 

The length of the game could've easily been half of what it was, and the final stretch of the game was really weird in the worst way. The game just wouldn't end and I hate to have felt that.

I will continue to be a fan of Analgesic because because they do a lot of experimental stuff so I'm glad this game exists in order to prove what works and what doesn't.

I can't say the game is bad bad, but it does it's best to sidestep it's potential. The concept of an open world adventure with secrets everywhere is mouthwatering, but the execution here is clumsy if not just lacking. The dreamcasty/ps2 style gameplay and graphics are nice, but the nostalgia is load bearing. Felt like everywhere I went that wasn't progress was a flat dead end designed to waste the player's time. Needs some quality of life improvements, and the contrast of deeply christian messaging with bisexual portrait art gave me whiplash.

If you're curious about the actual stats, check the achievements page for how many people finished the game (less than 1 in 5).

I want to preface this by saying that I am a big fan of both Anodyne 1 and 2. I went into this game with a positive attitude and a lot of excitement, and that makes some of what I am about to say... difficult. I am not a reviewer, but will occasionally put thoughts to screen if something moves me enough in one direction or the other. I'm only giving it a "no" because steam's rating system is binary and I can't give it a "This is extremely subjective, but my personal experience tilted pretty heavily toward the negative."

(The music is very good, and if nothing else you should buy the soundtrack to support the team!)

Before we get to anything else, I need to express that I have never before wanted a game to just END quite as much as I did during the back half of this game, and it just... stubbornly, WOULD. NOT. END. Well before I hit credits I was pleading for it, gasping for air, but it just kept going, and going, and going! Unspooling bizarre and disconnected plot beats and spinning them together as if in real time into entirely un-telegraphed and (with apology,) pretty hackneyed twists like it were bargaining for its life. "Just one more flashback, one more dream sequence, one more tortured allegory! Please, I'm not ready to go yet!" On and on it went, kicking and screaming, long after the overly-simplistic gameplay had been stretched far past the point of breaking, and well beyond the point that any catharsis could be wrung from its ridiculous conclusion aside from the hollow relief that follows a traumatic experience.

I may be (and am) stupid, but for the life of me I can't figure out whether or not the entire incoherent and rambling story that this game presents was meant to be told in good faith or as a cruel jab toward early PS1 RPGs, made at the player's expense. How much was allegorical? With how unreliable the narrator is, did ANY part of it actually happen as described? Was it just an excuse to engineer an increasingly-incoherent series of escalating last-minute twists? I don't know, but it makes an excellent case for games-as-art, because I was certainly feeling things by the end of it.
Perhaps this is the true Melos's trick.

I now need to address the gameplay, because in my opinion it is the game's most significant failing. Simple in theory, executed reasonably well, but without the mechanical depth or polish necessary to carry a game of its... ambition. You will end the game doing almost exactly what you started it doing, and that is not meant as a compliment. There are a meager handful of artifacts, gun parts, and alternate weapons you can use to mix up combat, but they don't do nearly enough to alter the basic gameplay sufficiently to keep things fresh. Combat starts to smell by the end of the second or third area, and it may as well be a century egg by the end.

Despite their simplicity, the movement controls are slippery and imprecise, which isn't great for a game that expects a weirdly high amount of precision from you. Jumping feels awful, you slide like you're on ice, and all types of collisions are unrelentingly jank. You will constantly be slipping and sliding and bouncing on and off of pretty much every surface except for the one you actually want to be on. And the game really starts to lean into this aspect, too. Standard, setting-appropriate level design gets thrown to the wayside SHOCKINGLY quickly, along with the more clever uses of searching within levels. In its place are a cavalcade of wacky contraptions and enemy setups designed not for challenge or atmosphere, but instead to inflict as much suffering and aggravation on the player as possible.

On the subject of misery, there's a kind of consistent, game-wide undercurrent of disdain for the player that only becomes readily apparent after you're already spent long enough to get invested in the goings-on of the world. It felt like a bait-and-switch, but I can't help but respect it. The game is full of various types of rude traps and unavoidable enemy setups and instant kills that might have been fun if not for the sloppy controls. Feels a little like a younger sibling thumbing you in the ribs whenever you start doing too well at street fighter. An endearing sort of mean-spirited that I can't get too mad at. I'm not a fan, but I know there's lots of folks out there that love that sort of thing.

It may sound like I'm making this game out to be, like, HARD hard, but it's less difficult and more... irritating? It's not hard for good reasons, I guess is what I mean. Unintended enemy attack pattern overlap and a general lack of reliable maneuverability make for a lot of unmanageable scenarios that would be significantly more manageable if the game wasn't so much the way that it is. It's nice enough to have an always-available difficulty selection if you're not playing the nightmare modes, and even has a soft form of dynamic difficulty on top of that. If you're doing well enough, it'll prompt you to increase the difficulty to the next available bracket, for a little extra challenge.

My recommendation? Do not do this! Choose a difficulty you're comfortable with and stay there. All high does is remove pickups other than money and make the enemies stronger, and the core gameplay is far too janky to justify inflated damage numbers. Unless you're the type of person who enjoys wasting their time as much as this game enjoys wasting yours, your time is better spent on normal, or easy if you only care about the unhinged plot.
You have been warned.

Now that we've gotten the negatives out of the way, I'd like to wholeheartedly praise the visual direction (and sound effect design but to a lesser degree), because it's an absolutely incredible homage to what it's going for. I don't think I've ever seen another game capture the spirit of PS1 vertex-shaded characters like this one. You can't exactly luxuriate in it the way it was intended due to the game kicking you in the teeth every time you feel like stopping to smell the roses, but it still makes for an exceptionally memorable experience, visually speaking. It's got a 4:3 mode and it's enabled by default! That's gutsy!

This is a great-looking, interesting, creative game that could easily have been several hours shorter to its exclusive benefit. It tries to do a lot, and I respect it a lot. How much of that it succeeds at will be left as an exercise for other players.

It's a game that I'll probably never return to, but definitely one I'll remember, for better or worse.

(Buy the soundtrack!)

I want to finish this game in hopes that I can discover the reasons for its (currently) high review, but the longer I play the less I'm enjoying it.

Exploration.  The game presents itself as having a focus on exploration and discovery but this is shallow, to the point of lying.  I'm over a couple of areas into the game and all of the overworld levels have been fairly obvious to their location, there was never any feeling of reward in discovering a level.  What about within a level?  No.  Your reward for exploration within levels is fluff.  At best you get a pittance of currency, but far too frequently you are instead punished for your exploration.  Your possible rewards in this game are money, health, food, enemies, traps, or some form of nothing, such as making a tree appear.  While there is some semblance of equipment, I have never received such from thorough exploration of a level, only from towns or events.  Money has been largely superfluous so far, with available purchases mostly being upgrades I am not interested in, or resources for some time later in the game.  Food would be okay if not for the limited inventory, it's ability to spoil (an arbitrary and frustrating mechanic), and the way it feels like a bandage for the awful combat, more on that later.  None of these are worth the too common risk of instead discovering danger.  In short, for all of my time and effort to thoroughly explore my environment in this self-proclaimed exploration-focused game, I am only the worse for it.  Going forward I feel zero incentive to do anything beyond head for the sign-posted goal at the end of the level.

Combat.  I hate the combat. It sucks. I don't want to do it. But it is 90% of the content in this game.  While there is novelty and possibly nostalgia in the "bumpslash" combat, it is fundamentally limited.  You have to approach and make contact with an enemy to attack it.  When every enemy has some gimmick this makes the combat, at best, tedious and annoying.  Every enemy is shooting you with projectiles which point-blank are nearly unavoidable, or is teleporting about, or is actively running away from you.  Individually, these are not terrible or unexpected.  Obviously the enemies have mechanics.  But every level is a gauntlet.  Every level is just room after room of several very annoying enemies.  This is all the worse due to the knockback you suffer from actually hitting something.  So many encounters turn into a bullet hell where every enemy also serves as a bumper you rebound off of.  I am constantly frustrated by a perceived lack of agency.  The art style and camera give a poor sense of space making it hard to maneuver vertically some times.  Enemies have arbitrary hurt-boxes; if an enemy's only method of attack is shooting projectiles, why does it need to inflict contact damage for accidentally landing on it?  And especially frustrating is when enemies are randomly invincible for the sake of the encounter.  Want to take out the pest shooting at you from across the room?  Too bad, unlike every other time you've encountered this same, visually-identical enemy, this time it can't be killed for the sake of the "combat experience."  The gun you acquire is ineffectual, distracting, and largely worthless for its limited capacity which is only replenished through the above bumpslash combat.  I do not have the prose to describe all of the faults and shortcomings and frustrations of the combat and convey how much I am disliking it.  What I can say is that I am disappointed and a little betrayed that the game is mostly an endless series of similar levels, all but a few of which being gauntlets of enemies in a game with miserable combat.  I've completed maybe 20 "optional" levels, and only like 2 were the least bit memorable, not to say that they weren't also annoying.

Story.  I haven't seen enough of the story to weigh in on it appropriately.  I have currently only completed the starting area and the mine.  So far, however, I am struggling to care about the story being presented.  The main characters so far all feel very flat with nebulous motives.  When starting on any of the major objectives the player is presented with a snippet of story relating to a character centric to this objective.  The main character somehow suddenly knows this character and their plight.  I do not care about these characters.  I do not know how deeply it was intended for me to care about them, but just being explained their life story does not move me.  As a result I do not feel involved in these narratives.  Events happen but there are never "twists" or "developments" because I never got to know or care about these characters in the first place.  I can't be "betrayed" if I never, as a player, trusted them.  It has made these threads feel shallow.

Misc.  The main character's design is ridiculous.  The collared shirt + tie + armor all in crop top is insane, and rather than come across as unique or charming I just hate it and the character by extension.  I don't know if it's meaning lost in translation or just being distastefully cryptic, but so much dialogue feels off.  I'm already fighting every other aspect of the game, I cannot be asked to dig through a sludge of dialogue to figure out intents and nuance.  Expanding on secrets being harmful to the point of maliciousness: there is a secret in the first town that leads to a difficult area.  The area was seemingly not intended to be tackled so early, but because it was available I did it.  My reward for accomplishing something above my bracket was nothing appropriately powerful but the same reward as any of the starting areas, and then getting dumped into the overworld 3 areas away from where I was supposed to be with no ready means or returning thus needing to progress backwards through harder areas.

tl;dr:  The game isn't bereft of things to like.  The music is fine and the art design is (mostly) good.  But these all pale before the mountain of complaints I have for the game.  I want to like it.  There seem to be a good number of people who do (some of who even have more than 3 hours in the game).  But for reasons I can't well articulate, nearly every part of the game is fighting me.  If I finish the game it very well may be out of spite.  For its retail price, try the demo and make sure this is a game you know you will like before buying.

I can't really recommend this game. I played it after watching an early access review for it with the mechanics of overworld exploration and bump-slashing combat. It does those fairly well and if that was the whole game I would recommend it.

Unfortunately the story is totally unhinged. It goes from fantasy exploration to christian narrative to ww2 narrative to god knows what. It can't keep it's tone straight and I have no idea what story it's trying to tell. The story really turned me off of the game.
This game was really fun for awhile but then it just comes way too repetitive and the last bit is just terrible design with no actual saving.

I can't say the game is bad bad, but it does it's best to sidestep it's potential. The concept of an open world adventure with secrets everywhere is mouthwatering, but the execution here is clumsy if not just lacking. The dreamcasty/ps2 style gameplay and graphics are nice, but the nostalgia is load bearing. Felt like everywhere I went that wasn't progress was a flat dead end designed to waste the player's time. Needs some quality of life improvements, and the contrast of deeply christian messaging with bisexual portrait art gave me whiplash.

I found the controls to be very frustrating at places.  I fell in hole that was necessary to do a level.  You can't control it in the hole. Another platforming level has these pink things that knock you off the ledges.  The jumping is impossible because there are 30 of them in a row and if you miss one you have to start over.  The levels don't tell you when they are done. Like on one level I got into this church and lit up 3 flowers.  Was that all I was supposed to do there?  Also when walk up to a level marker it doesn't say if you finished it or not.

I wanna preface this by saying I really enjoyed a lot of what this game has to offer. My enjoyment began to dwindle as the pretty nonsensical story and weirdness started to overshadow the fun mystery and exploration. The final third of the game is especially egregious for this, with exploration officially ending, and the game becoming a lot of strange dialogue and exposition, mixed with a handful of boss fights (some good, some not so good)

I think there are many really cool ideas present in the game, and it brought that old school zelda exploration vibe. I would've really enjoyed seeing that aspect of the game expanded on, with npc's being less one note, and the world itself having a bit more life.

The first few hours of this game is really good, sets an amazing tone and the gameplay is solid.

But after the three hour mark it sort of hits you like a brickwall, "Is this it?"
You always feel like you're missing some sort of tool, a piece of tech, a forgotten mechanic of sorts but no you didn't, you just always feel like that; and because of that feeling the game gets really old real fast.
The "Find levels" mechanic is neat, but it's nothing particularly amazing or fun,  it's just something to make things feel a bit more tedious. Some of the levels are really cool, fun atmosphere and mechanics, but the mines suck. 
GOD I HATE THE MINES. LIKE MAN, WHAT WERE YOU THINKING?
Also, the music feels so soft and non-existent, I couldn't even pick out a song that stands out to me if I could.

The plot of the story is fascinating at the start, but at some point it stops feeling coherent and drops the ball entirely, changing into something else that really drags on too long and makes you want to exclaim, "WRAP IT UP PLEASE".

Look, I liked Anodyne 1 and 2, masterpieces in my eyes but this? I don't like it, and it's not like I didn't put effort into it, but it's rough on all fronts and felt like a chore to drag myself through, I cannot recommend it to anyone because even if I did, they probably wouldn't want to finish it.
Nostalgic in a not fun way if that makes sense

Peppered is a quirky and simple platformer that brings a great concept with poor execution.

I love the concept of this game. A platforming game where you get to choose your own story in an apocalypse setting sounds awesome. Unfortunately that's about where the fun ends.

The story is great, I wish it was a visual novel. The platforming just didn't work out for me. It felt extremely simple and reminded me of old flash games. The mechanics are pretty limited and the difficulty is essentially non existent as there is a checkpoint almost every screen.

That's honestly my main problem with the game. The characters are fun and the dialogue is silly, though sometimes a little obnoxious. I don't really know how else to say it other than it's very millennial dialogue? (I'm sorry) I really enjoyed my first 2ish hours but after that I quickly lost interest in the game.

TL;DR: Fun concept and story with boring platforming and funny/annoying dialogue depending on your humor.
art was nice, gameplay and story was meh, buy if you like nice visuals.

I do not agree with the overwhelmingly positive attitude of the "review score" for this boomer shooter boss fight game with light action "RPG" elements. 
 
+ During the starting two hours the Atmosphere of the muddy, slimy and primal ooze feeling world is great and curious to begin with. (I think in my case this is helped by recently having watched loads of videos on how the world looked and "felt" hundreds of millions of years ago in the pre-human world, but that is a tangent that I am going to stop right here. Its just fit into this) 

+ The mix between goofy characters and their designs and starkly dark vibes and undertones that both enemies and allies give is just weirdly compelling. The faces that especially some of the bosses pull during fights are premium creep me out territory.   

= Movement seems off, sluggish and inaccurate at times. I am putting this in neutral because I did not have the issues with platforming that others are reporting and I feel that this is less of an issue with the movement and more caused by issues mentioned further below. 

= Music and sound design are all over the place. Some of the music is compelling but then there are tracks which consist of short beepy loops that are overstaying their REALLY fast. Getting rid of hearing the music of the beginner area was more of a driving force then the actual game. The games Weapons very much suffer under subpar sound design. Which isn't helped by...

- Two hours in the three weapons that I got (starting pistol with infinite ammo, shotgun and smg) feel weak and ineffectual. For being a central pillar of the gameplay that is pretty bad. 

Right now I completely ignore the SMG because it is too weak and its ammo is gone too fast. more importantly using the SMG is time that I am not reloading the much more useful starting gun with its endless ammunition. 

The shotgun is there to take down really close enemies and useless on any other distance. The problem being that it is too slow to reload its two shots per "magazin", to slow to fire and that even close up has problems taking out enemies effectively by either being to weak or having to far spread. So overall I simply try avoiding this gun as well.  

I get that the idea that you are supposed to change between weapons for the appropriate situation but when EVERY weapons has the same weaknesses I will end up using the gun with the least issues. The Starting gun has okay reload times, okay damage on all distances and endless ammo. So why wouldn't I stick with it during the already difficult boss fights. I want to mention that Doom Eternal also has a focus on switching weapons, with far more weapons then I have found in this game at this point and I have much less problems with this then in this game. 

I feel the core problem here are the (relatively) long reload times. If the guns would reload while you do not have them in your hand, it would immensely bolster the player to switch between weapons.

- Collision boxes are BAD. You are constantly being hit by attacks that look and feel like they shouldn't have hit you. It doesn't help that this seems to mostly concern enemies that are the most chunky and fast. This has affected bosses and mid sized enemies the most so far. I literally had a boss attack pass me by, visually clearly not connecting with me, and still got damaged.

- Enemies during boss fights, both bosses and adds constantly run at you with speed that is seemingly miss matched with the players ability to move and evade them. Together with the issues around hitboxes I feel I am constantly hit by shit I shouldn't be or in situations where I feel there was nothing I could have done to avoid the hit or being boxed in due to the insufficient damage output of my weapons or the lack of movement options. 

I feel compelled to try this game more due to its atmosphere, characters and visual design.
I can see myself try and learn to love the game around its weaknesses and it still might click with you BUT I just do not think that this, at all, deserves the often unreserved positive ratings here on Steam as I find core aspects of its design and gameplay severely lacking in essential polish.

I am going to come back to this at some point but for now I am going to put this back onto my "to play" shelve while there are much less flawed, very challenging shooters in my back catalogue waiting for a play.

Together with the issues around hitboxes I feel I am constantly hit by shit I shouldn't be or in situations where I feel there was nothing I could have done to avoid the hit or being boxed in due to the insufficient damage output of my weapons or the lack of movement options. 

I feel compelled to try this game more due to its atmosphere, characters and visual design.
I can see myself try and learn to love the game around its weaknesses and it still might click with you BUT I just do not think that this, at all, deserves the often unreserved positive ratings here on Steam as I find core aspects of its design and gameplay severely lacking in essential polish.

I am going to come back to this at some point but for now I am going to put this back onto my "to play" shelve while there are much less flawed, very challenging shooters in my back catalogue waiting for a play.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Beat the game with max difficulty (full 2.0). It's a good but frustrating 'souls-like' game.

[b]PROS[/b]

[b]Music[/b]       10
[b]Visuals[/b]     8
[b]Platforming[/b] 8
[b]Powerup[/b]     9 - By utilizing skills effectively, it's possible to reach higher places early.
[b]Map[/b]         10 - Completed area are marked blue, allows easy 100%.

[b]CONS[/b]

[b]Exploring[/b]     5 - Every venture is a life-or-death situation, making it to the next checkpoint is not easy. No way to overpower yourself by exploring/overlevelling.
[b]Crowd control[/b] 3 - Aside from a few ultimate attacks, no way to clear mobs safely. Not easy to fill the gauge.
[b]Mob design[/b]    3 - Almost all mobs has backstep and iframes, very annoying. You are not the main character, everyone has main character syndrome.
[b]Boss design[/b]   4 - Bosses has telegraphed attacks (good), most are multi-phased (neutral), and has built-in iframe backstep (bad). Bosses moves relative to player: the faster you move, the faster boss becomes. Imagine Elden Ring DLC boss PCR where instead of his original moveset he uses Lightspeed Slash and Rock Sling all the time.

In 'difficult' games like Elden Ring and Monster Hunter, player can become stronger by knowledge and strategic planning. Such opportunity does not exist in this game, the only way is to git gud.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
My decision to critique Ender Magnolia comes at a moment when I'm personally overwhelmed and disappointed by the increasingly rote aspects of design I'm experiencing in contemporary metroidvanias... and just the sheer number of them. As such, your delight and excitement with this game will likely hinge on how much you're willing to engage with, or directly enjoy, grinding, parrying and dodging. While the majority of my hours with the game proved to be a lot of fun learning the lay of the land, by the 13-hour mark the difficulty spikes with back-to-back bosses, my increasing disinterest in piecemeal plotting made me decide to set the game aside. And this was after I experimented with different artifact builds and difficulty settings outside of the normal presets - which, to the game's credit, they offer an exceptionally robust set of sliders to tweak just about every aspect of the game to your liking. 

Ender Magnolia began as an intriguing adventure with fast action and a learning curve that felt good and challenging. But for me, when everything in a game begins to feel like a chore, the immersive experience I'm looking for, essentially flies out the window. I enjoyed core aspects of the game enough that I may go back and play the first one if on sale, but if you're a gamer who is getting wary of metroidvania's seemingly complete embrace of the Souls formula without much original refinement, I offer an additional word of caution.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: It's not terrible, it's not bad, it's just mediocre. A disappointment compared to Ender Lilies.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
I'd give it a neutral if I could, but this game didn't really grab me. Combat is a little floaty. Backgrounds are nice, but the animation is a little sub-par. Getting around feels a little imprecise and the climbing animations are just the character sort of juddering to the top of things.

I think if you really like anime or the extended universe, or absolutely have to play every metroidvania then give it a go - but to me overwhelmingly positive shouldn't be how this game is rated, so I'm giving it a thumbs down for now.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Bought this (and Ender Lilies) because of the overwhelmingly positive reviews, but found it to personally to be a very middling experience, and it left me disappointed. A mixed recommendation would go well here, but since that's not possible I have to pick between positive and negative, and I decided to go with negative.

This game feels very lethargic to play. Jumps and dodges are slow to start and don't cover a lot distance. Combat feels unsatisfying and unresponsive, enemies don't react to your attacks unless you stagger them and attack tells are difficult to discern, mostly because of what is, I think, very lackluster audio and visual design. Not to say the game looks and sounds bad, I think the music and visuals are its strongest aspects, but this effort didn't seem to translate at all into making the combat feel good.

The game looks like a painting in motion, for better and for worse. This means it's nice to look at, but it does a poor job at conveying things visually to the player. Enemy tells are obfuscated by all the shiny visual effects from your own attacks and I can't tell you how many times I was losing HP from the burning status effect and didn't notice because visually it's barely noticeable.

These problems are also present in the audio design. The sound effects that accompany enemy (and your own) attacks are barely hearable. I tried turning down the music and ambience and I still could not rely on audio cues for anything.

These two problems lead to combat feeling so limp.

Overall, I didn't like the game, ultimately because its two core aspects, movement and combat, both felt unsatisfying and not fun. If you care about these things in games, you might not like it either. If you don't, or you don't find issue with this game's particular "feel", you'll probably like it.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: This is a hard one for me. Aesthetically, the game is beautiful. The visuals are stunning and the map is much more functional. Unfortunately, the game doesn't seem to have the soul of Ender Lilies. I was drawn into the first game by the dark story and empty world - the decay and rot and loneliness, the desire to find out what happened to the world - it was all done so well. Ender Magnolia tries this, but just doesn't succeed. Part of this may be that the enemies seem to be all machines? I'm not sure. It's missing heart. It feels empty somehow. I may finish it eventually, but I'd much rather play another run of Ender Lilies. This all being said, I do hope they make another one - clearly I'm in the minority here and the world they've created is something I'd like to explore again.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: I played through this to the end after playing ender lillies, and it just doesn't have the same feel, Most notably the music is absolutely depressing and repetitive, it's absolutely horrid up untill upper sanctum, and even then it's somber.. music can make or break a game, and this game it broke it.  I really hate it but the game is very pretty and handles well but the music completely made it a .... horrible and depressing experience that i wish i had missed, kinda like last of us p2.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
I love the last game, love the plot of this game but for some reason it has controller issues with ps4 and ps5 controllers. I'm not entirely sure why, it works for a period of time then all of a sudden the controls stop working. As an example the buttons just stop working, input lag, character walking in the wrong direction.

I play other games with a controller and I don't have an issue, I played their previous title without any controller issues but for some strange reason I keep getting controller issues with this game.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Gameplay is sluggish and floaty, didn't feel "right", when I was given in the beginning the double jump and dash for "free" instead of exploration rewards, it gave me the impression that was something half baked, if it is the initial moveset why even have a section of the game without said double jump and dash. The only thing this game does right is not having contact damage.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Saw it till the end but the gameplay is just too frictionless. There is little sense of exploration because the map automatically marks every progression gate, shows area completion status and even tells you when you have the correct ability to progress right from the start. Map markers are entirely useless and exploration overall feels automatic/checkboxy. Fast travel is always active and there is no real sense of danger of losing progress.

Atmosphere-wise the game feels a bit hollow somehow, can't really put my finger on it.

Combat feels nice after the early game, but the bosses could have had trickier attacks up their sleeves especially closer to the end.

Can't comment on the story, skipped it entirely in both games but reading other reviews I am glad I did.

Overall I cannot really recommend getting this unless you have played the plethora of games that are more competent in their execution of the genre, including Lilies

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
ENDER MAGNOLIA is the sequel of ENDER LILLIES. I enjoyed the original game more than the sequel. The graphical and musical styles stayed pretty much the same between these two games, and I am fine with that. Unfortunately the gameplay has not evolved at all and playing through EM felt like a repeat of EL, but somehow worse. 

EDIT: About performance, the game is badly optimized and doesn't work on lower end machines. A laptop that is good enough for Dark Souls and Hollow Knight: Silksong should be able to run this game imo, but the reality is that older laptops just can not handle this game.

The abilities you get in this game are basically the same as in EL, so the developer didn't have to come up with new ideas. They just rehashed them. The enemy design felt very much the same. I did not count the enemy variety in EM. It almost felt like the most boring enemy designs from EL were just copied over and that was that. It's been a hot moment since I've played EL, but I still remember some of the enemy and boss designs from that game and prefer them over EM ones.

The map had maybe 2-3 areas that I liked and 1 area that I disliked with a passion. The graphics of all areas were nice, but especially the "suddenly Japan" area felt like it didn't belong to the game. Some areas were unfortunately very forgetful. 

The story was told in a similar style as EL, but either I just wasn't invested in it at all, or EL managed to tell the player key elements of the story better without having to pay a lot of attention to the notes you find around the world. There were some clear connections to the previous game, but I feel like the depth of the connections could've been better. Somehow the stories feel very disconnected from each other. NOTE: This could just be that I was so disinterested in the story that something completely obvious escaped me making me feel this way.

Getting 100% completion takes maybe around 20 hours or less if you are actually good at the game. 

If you haven't played ENDER LILLIES somehow and are looking at this game, go play it first. If after that you feel like the game could've used a DLC or so, this game could be for you.

Pros
- Fun characters
- Good aesthetic
- It's like Ace Attorney, but not complete garbage.
- Nearly every "the dev is an asshole" game mechanic (time limited decisions) can be disabled in the settings

Cons
- Extremely repetitive writing. Game reiterates information at least six times, which is both patronizing and tedious making it a slog getting through even a single scene. Many conversations go on three times as long as they need to, and the lead character's internal monologue could be cut entirely and you wouldn't miss anything.
- Bright lights and chromatic abberation cause very nasty eye strain.

Many conversations go on three times as long as they need to, and the lead character's internal monologue could be cut entirely and you wouldn't miss anything.
- Bright lights and chromatic abberation cause very nasty eye strain.

While I love the idea of playing a Pathfinder 2e video game, this just isn't it. It feels like almost every fight was designed by the world's most adversarial DM you've ever dealt with. There's almost no such thing as "just an encounter." Near every fight is either a severe encounter or the enemies have so many advantages stacked on their side that it might as well be one. The main campaign isn't awful with this but it isn't terribly enjoyable, even with an optimized party. But the free battle mode and the DLC just goes completely off the rails.

Level 2 encounters where enemies can throw out 50+ damage per shot while being nigh impossible to hit due to layered bonuses. Numerous fights that have gone on 5+ rounds of just whiffing attacks because the enemies have stacked bonuses that prevent you hitting them. Multiple fights where you have highly restrictive terrain and almost no way to work around it if you didn't know it was coming and bring a solution with you.

Just every sort of encounter that if you dealt with it at an actual tabletop you would leave to find a better DM.

I just want to set expectations for those who are tolerant of minimalist graphics in search of hidden gems. There are plenty of things that are well-implemented about this game, but it seems to be little more than a turn-based encounter simulator that uses Pathfinder rules. You just sorta go from predetermined encounter to encounter in a straight line. Encounter, fight, win, rest, repeat.

There's also not a lot of personality. It feels a bit like sitting down to a table of people who don't really understand how to roleplay, but are giving it their best: "Hey, Zorduk, don't forget that your +1 Longsword gives you a reaction bonus against half-orcs!" "Ah, yes, thank you Astalfia, I had almost forgotten that the divine Oculon gives me that ability once per short rest."

I wouldn't rule out going back to this game, and having bought it on sale I can hardly say I feel cheated, but I would expect there to be a bit more here, like, I don't know, exploring a dungeon.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
So I played for a while now. TLDR: not there yet, but might have potential. Feels like pre-alpha.

I am totally fine with being cozy but the game introduces levels and skills which are so grindy I get flashbacks to the very first mmos. 
You level harvesting to harvest a bit faster and a number goes up. To harvest you click on the harvestable environment which is always in the exact same location.
Now replace harvesting with any other skill and that is about it.

This needs a lot of work in terms of story or progression to have a reason to be.

There is no life in entities. Cows, sheep, dragons and your little helpers all just exist and stare at you. No interaction. Just like a t-posed placeholder.

Performance is aweful. I have a solid system 9800X3D + RX9070XT and GPU is running higher than cyberpunk on epic with noisy coils and all. This is inacceptable considering how the game looks, feels and that the world is tiny. Like run across in 30 seconds.

So yeah... I wish the game the best, but it has a long way to go.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: Bumped up the res as high as it would go. 4k. dint seem to change anything, graphics are blurry. Slightly unfocused. Game play video is actually better.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
As far as early access games go, it feels very fleshed out. Well made, clearly a lot of thought put into the idea, and time put into the development/updates! The reviews speak for themselves, that is why I purchased based off of the largely positive reviews overall.

However, you are kind of forced to explore the game's Discord server in order to get quick assistance, have your content seen if you want to make suggestions. Posts can/will be deleted/pruned if you broke even the slightest of rules including spoiler faux-pas (what constitutes a spoiler anyway? staff decide).

I will continue playing (as I have indeed paid for this product and that is my right). I can't in good faith leave a positive recommended review. Watching guidelines so I will say no more.

Possibly better to avoid the third party Discord server and stick to Steam for discussions/help/suggestions, etc.

Best of luck.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
It definitely fits the bill of being a cozy shop sim, but I don't understand how it got the score that is has. The map is filled with a *lot* of visual glitches (floating trees, assets not actually connected to things, etc.). Most other aspects are okay at best, but all of it is very unpolished (early access to be fair). On top of that I lost an hour or so of progress despite the game definitely saying it was saved.

It could eventually get to a better state that's worth the price, but right now it's far from it.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: I think this could be great, but I felt like there were a lot of bugs (some that I would expect for early access, then a few big ones that I wouldn't have expected). I love the look of the environment, and it has the core grindy gameplay there which I liked. Some parts feel a bit cobbled together, there is a mismatch of style with some of the asset packs that have been used which was emersion breaking at times/didn't really fit with the rest of the world. It looks very similar to Ale&Tale.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
I tried posting this in the Discord earlier and people immediately got defensive. Honestly, that’s a clear reason to stay away from the game.

Let me preface this by saying I love the idea behind it and the concepts.


Critique

First and foremost, the game seems to be suffering an identity crisis.

Is it a shop sim? Is it primarily a decoration game? Is it open world exploration? It can certainly do all three of these together. Other games have for sure. But right now, the systems feel too disjointed instead of coming together in harmony.

For me, the biggest way this is shown is through the substantial item bloat.

Variations and large amounts of distinct items aren't inherently bad. 

However, the way it's presented here seems like it's getting pulled in 3 diff directions.

Exploration aspect - There are a TON of different resources. However, they’re more like recolors of each other. All the ore does the same thing. All the crystals do the same thing. All the berries do the same thing. Yet there are at least 10 variations of each. The berries are the most numerous example because each season has around 5 variations of berries. The ore and crystals remain the same throughout the seasons.

	All berries can be either used in jam or in a non-alcoholic drink when combined with honey. All berries can be found in abundance with no rarity difference. For the ore, they can all get smelted into ingots. I’ll get more into it with the shop simulation part. For the crystals, there are some that can be gotten only through mini events that happen in the world. The crystals can either be turned into trinket-like items or they can be turned into aether. Aether can be used for decoration and applied to your crops. 

Shop aspect - On the Discord FAQs, the dev explained how pricing works. Simply put, you are better off selling at market price or below. There’s no incentive to sell it above market price. Even if it’s a .50 markup. Again, the dev clearly stated there is no “min-maxing”, but at the same time, it feels very restrictive. So, why even give us the option to adjust the price? It seems like the option was tacked on like it was marking a checkbox of what a shop sim should have. 

	As for the item bloat I mentioned, it ties into what I mentioned above. Different products will have different market prices. That’s a given. However, with such a variation of items, you’d think that’d lend itself to being an incentive to experimenting with pricing. But as I said, you really can’t. Certain things will have a higher market price.


Decoration aspect - Ultimately, this is what I think the game comes down to. It’s a decoration sim primarily instead of a shop simulator like what the game title says. But…it’s even restrictive with that. 

	You can craft products like the trinket-like items I mentioned above. However, you can’t actually decorate your space with them. I tried to put one of the trinkets I liked on the counter and….I couldn’t. You’re restricted to shelves, lighting, books, walls, paintings, potted plants, and holiday items from what I saw. 

	If we could decorate our shop and area with items we crafted, that’d be freaking amazing! It’d give incentive to craft different jams, gem trinkets, ores, etc aside from selling them. But, you can’t. 

Now, hearing that the dev is censoring people? I’m not surprised. 

I fell for the emotional manipulation and can’t get my money back. Oh well, one less Chipotle meal for me then. 

Avoid.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
There are a lot of things I'd like to see before I can recommend this game.
Because in it's current state, it's extremely bland.

The gameplay loop right now, is running around the same map you ran around the previous in game day to slowly hold click over various materials, animals, rocks, fruit, etcetera. Take all that stuff back to your shop, and then slowly hold over shelves, crafting benches, etcetera.
As you level you gather things faster, but levelling takes ages... like MMO levels of time. Like, after 11 hours, it still takes me ages to gather stone. It's not fun. It needs some serious tuning.

And that's it... You can do the cashier stuff yourself, but that gets old really fast. So get a ball of light and a... badger? To do shelf stacking and cashier stuff.

The organisation of your bag of stuff is appalling. Like, there's a search tool, but you need to know what things are called. Have an example: You take honey and you put it into a brewer... Okay, the results of which are... I tried mead, brew, syrup.... Until I had to scroll through the list, thankfully not far, it's alphabetical. "Classic Buzz"... So I couldn't exactly guess that. But the category is... shelf. Just everything that goes on shelves. Which same for the catalogue you buy from, is just too vast.

Alternatively, decoration. For which there is no search bar. Let's say you want a table. There are two items called Small Table and they are at opposite ends of the list. So one is near the start, easy to find. The other is past jackolanterns, grave stones, candles, floor mats, signs, banners, books, paintings, cups, cuttlery, walls... This list is not alphabetical. This list is just a mess.

There are mounts. But I don't know if we can call them that. They are just assets that float below you if you look down. I chose a frog, it has no animations for movement at all. It's just a floating frog. 

The customers look pretty cool. Which makes the fact that the player is just a floating ball of light, extremely disappointing. Be nice if there was a character creation.

One of my biggest issues of the game is it's fast travel... It's not a fast travel. There are "Magic Ziplines" You have to point your crosshair at them, which if they're far away is going to be hard. Thankfully you can place them yourself... unfortunately you cannot use them when carrying them. So if you're making a chain of fast travel points to go from one area to another, you have to carry them all, ONE AT A TIME, across the entire map. This takes a very long time.
Then there's fog... Which stops you seeing them at all. Thanks game, that didn't take a whole hour or anything!

Customisation is a bit lacking. You can't move your counter, you can't add doors (You can jump out windows, but jumping back in is difficult.) There's a floating block of something that gives you 10 X your level in gold each day. can't move that. I put books under it to stop it looking like it was floating.
There's a picture of a dog, (A Pomeranian, I think.) that you cannot move or get rid of. (Sorry, I dislike dogs.) There's a globe, a dice and a sword in a stone, that you cannot move. 

It takes a VERY long time to unlock the entire building. I'd put things around to make gathering everything a lot easier, I was selling all the best stuff. The game was just waiting at this point. And my gods... it's a lot of waiting.

Not everything is bad about the game... it's just very very slow. I genuinely think the game shows a lot of promise and some day I hope to update this to a positive review.
But as it is right now... I really cannot recommend it at all. Much as I'd like to, the developer seems pretty friendly.
The game is just a bit... bland.

Try it for yourself though, maybe you'll enjoy it more than I do.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: Performance is terrible, how this game is so positive. Running a 7800XT with 5800X3D. I get that my system is a bit old now but surely this game should run ok?

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
Teddy’s Haven gets old fast. Day 1 plays exactly the same as day 100, with no meaningful progression, challenge, or variation to keep things interesting. Once you’ve seen the core loop, you’ve seen the entire game.

The “crafting” system is especially bad — shallow, unrewarding, and barely deserves to be called crafting at all. It feels tacked on rather than thought through.

To top it off, the game has a very strong AI-generated feel. Assets, presentation, and overall design lack cohesion and originality, giving the impression that quantity was prioritised over quality.

Ironically, I’m writing this review using AI — much like it feels the developer used AI for every asset in the game.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
This is what passes for "Overwhelmingly Positive" is it?

The modelling work is good, but everything else is amateurish jank.  Movement is...basic.  Animations are barely animated.  Sound design is basically nonexistent.  

It's just basic, bland, childish nothing.  And I have to say, the whimsy of a magical world is lost when you have to work register.  Seriously, counting change?  That's the kind of thing a 6 year old thinks is fun because they haven't had to do it for an 8 hour shift with a manager watching over their shoulder.

It feels like it was just churned out as fast as possible, ticking as many boxes as it could, but has no soul or personality to it.  Too bad.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
As far as early access games go, it feels very fleshed out. Well made, clearly a lot of thought put into the idea, and time put into the development/updates! The reviews speak for themselves, that is why I purchased based off of the largely positive reviews overall.

However, you are kind of forced to explore the game's Discord server in order to get quick assistance, have your content seen if you want to make suggestions. Posts can/will be deleted/pruned if you broke even the slightest of rules including spoiler faux-pas (what constitutes a spoiler anyway? staff decide).

I will continue playing (as I have indeed paid for this product and that is my right). I can't in good faith leave a positive recommended review. Watching guidelines so I will say no more.

Possibly better to avoid the third party Discord server and stick to Steam for discussions/help/suggestions, etc.

Best of luck.

game_name: HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats
review_text: This game is definitely one of the more challenging hidden cat games. I would go into detail as to why I disliked the DLC map, but Guerilla Grue did an excellent job of explaining the issues, so go read their review next. Just know that, by the end of it, I found myself clicking on every vaguely cat-shaped blob (good thing the game doesn't penalize you for too many incorrect clicks) and using every hint once the timer was up (and the game would pause if you were in another window, so you HAD to keep the game in the foreground while you waited).

game_name: HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats
review_text:
New negative review:
Dev deleted my community posts because they can't face the truth. This whole freemium honey pot scheme was crated to push new "updates" as paywalled DLCs. They are talking half truths even in their announcements to make people buy the DLC. Instead of being honest and saying they created a new DLC you can buy, they say to enjoy the new level of the game, that is clearly not available as a normal game update.

Old positive review:
+ Simple gameplay and progression, similar to Find All 1
+ Like the Find All 3 & 4, it has a timer-based hinting system, useful in finding that last missing cat
+ Fun, but not as fun as Find All series
+ Relaxing music
+ FREE
+ GNU/Linux support
+ no 3rd party spyware EULA

- no Steam Cloud
- the quality of the drawing was a bit annoying: some proportions didn't seem right and some cats looked a bit too sketchy for my taste. Some cats didn't look like cats at all, more like dogs and foxes; there was one cat that seemed like a bat
- the cat meows are quite boring and repetitive. It felt like they only recorded one meow and then just changed the speed to make it sound different. Cats have a wide range of "vocabulary", they could have added some purrs and fighting meows, at least
- for a free game, I shouldn't complain much, but I don't like the freemium schemes, luring people into a game, then give more content than the original through a DLC. This game was designed like that from the start, both the free game and the DLC were released on the same day. The DLC is pretty cheap, but let's say it was not quite what I was expected. I'll make a DLC review separately

Overall, HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats is a good game, it's also free (for real) and you can unlock all the achievements without buying the DLC. I'd say the DLC is more for the hard core fans of the hidden objects genre.

Cool and cute game that is all about managing "wild" side of a dragon. 

My negative review is due to the fact that there is NO GALLERY / CG / EVENT's pertaining to the "nichijou" or everyday life with the dragon. There is no cutscenes or still images pertaining how the "trainer" bonds or spends time with the dragon. 

In other games that are Visual Novels player would unlock certain routes and be rewarded with short segment accompanied with CG's that will show how main protagonist grows closer to the chosen heroine. THERE IS NOTHING LIKE THAT HERE. 

It's MENU simulator. There is very little interactions with the "dragon" and her responses are also very limited. 
What a shame.

Wall running and wall climbing require the same input and the margin for error is razor thin, making it unbelievely frustrating when you lose a line because of it. Until the mechanics behind both inputs are either fundamentally changed, or made clearer, I can't recommend this game.

I really really liked this game especially before the 1.0 patch, however there are design and mechanic issues I dont particularly agree with

1. Trick Rush being fundamentally unfun and flawed
I dont think having a shrinking circle and asking the player to get a big combo is good gameplay, it's very restricting and forces the player to stick to a very small circle and plays against the insanely tight combo multiplier that demands you to constantly do tricks to sustain that combo (the multiplier runs out mid-air too so you have to be smart about it), call it skill issue but its not exactly fun to be already at half of the gold point threshold with your previous combo, and then having to catch up with another combo thats equally as big with the allowed area reduced significantly, so you have to awkwardly stick to an even smaller area or a cheesy spot because this parkour system isnt made with sudden and precise turning implemented (your character carries some sort of momentum so you cant exactly jolt them the other direction asap without breaking flow), I think it can be improved in these ways:

-remove the shrinking circle but keep the limited cone of area, better if its a square tbh
you can argue this removes some of the challenge, but my other suggestions can bring it back to a level that, in my opinion, can be more fun and compliments the established combo system
-increase point threshold or decrease timer
a easy basic fix that brings back some of the challenge
-sustaining the combo multiplier is what your score will depend on
 instead of being timer based, make it based on your multiplier and have the player try to achieve the score threshold with one score multiplier rather than a time limit. this would compliment the open area and force a player to be creative and plan out a route that will allow them to accrue the highest score in a single run, which addresses my main gripe in that trick rush is very restrictive and not fun and actively discourages player creativity.

2. The LT button
I think the LT button is kind of clunky and a little unintuitive, its very context sensitive and is sometimes frustrating to deal with, I think it should still allow the character to initiate a climb even if the wall isnt high enough to climb over so that it feels more consistent to use, as well as general issues with the camera being the deciding factor to whether or not a wall climb will turn into a wall run, but maybe thats the fault of the camera rather than the climb button itself, regardless there is jank and I think it could be improved, it also applies to ziplines too, where it demands you to time your zipline grab rather than it automatically happening when someone has the button held, which is very misleading as the moveset says to hold LT rather than to time and then hold, a nitpick but just something that makes it feel unintuitive.

3. The (Lack of) Ability to Modify Your Controls
This is a basic QOL thing thats very baffling to not have, the ability being able to change your keybinds and have options is such an essential thing for most games and it just sucks massively and detracts from this game to not have that option, especially since the binds for MnK are not exactly the best. As well as camera smoothing being there by default and no way to turn it off, its just honestly very limiting to not have the option to have it turned off, or not having a "toggle LT" mode, or just being allowed to bind your boost (which is necessary for combos) to anything BUT r3 (I find it these buttons very weird and jank to use).  Just very weird overall

4. Progression System, Combo System, and Extrinsic Value
I think having cosmetics tied to feathers/events that you complete is kind of ass, it turned me off from playing the game honestly, I was from the EA period and having stuff I technically already had and didn't need to grind for be walled off by events made me feel like I was being forced to play a certain way rather than allowing me to just enjoy the game and progress naturally because of it. Personal biases aside I think a better way to implement a progression system to this game is a leveling system rather than a set amount of tokens you acquire from challenges, and a leveling system that actually rewards you for merely PLAYING the game, giving Extrinsic Value and purpose to the big score at the top left of your screen and actually forcing the player to interact with the Combo System outside of the events as there is no real tangible reason/reward to doing so and it just kind of feels like an afterthought when you're just playing around the map. An endless leveling system could also serve as "reputation" for the community and a small reward to experience players who chose to master the system to the point where they have bragging rights. It's just an odd design choice to force players to engage with the trick rush and runs by locking off customization items that are ultimately not worth the grind (literally just two color slots and some only have one). I feel like the progression system should reward you for playing rather than completing a list, and if you wanted to incentivize people to play the challenges there should be a "weekly world leaderboard" thing or a special unique item that is only unlockable through those challenges, the leaderboard thing would absolutely work as most players will optimize things when there is competition involved.

anyways, this game has very solid and great base mechanics, outside from a little jank, the core gameplay mechanics are FUN, even if the combo window can be too tight (you have a boost that stops the timer so its fine). however there are many issues around it that actively detract from the game and makes it hollow and at times frustrating to play, some parts feel unrewarding when you play and interact with the game the way it wants you to.  Wish these could be improved as these issues turn a relaxing high-skill ceiling game into a quite frustrating experience.

I want to like this game, but it makes that hard to do. First of all, ziplines. A core part of some of the level design, and used in trial challenges. The most finnicky and tight stuff to activate. Sometimes you straight up won't grab it no matter how you time it or where at. I thought within range of the yellow cord and climbing, which, yes, worked. But would also decisively not work too. It also feels like no one tested these, as I cannot get a smooth run following the checkpoints. Almost as if any sort of failure was not accounted for. For example. Two, prime examples actually, both in Sunset Paradise. An alley run and ground/rooftop run. In the Alley run, there is red wall that you wall jump to, and are supposed to wall run along. A majority of the time, you click to climbing a rooftop ledge that is out of view during this. The checkpoints lead you this high. The second one has you jump down into the plaza to start, and has you wall eject up to a rooftop. You can't. You can, but not at all in a smooth and steady run. You get two jumps in and the architecture of the two adjacent buildings angles inward and makes you drop. I can't get a single bronze in trials because of stuff like this. I am following instructions. And it feels like I just shouldn't be doing any of this here. Like the map just barely fits the given purpose. I feel better doing my own thing than the trials, because none of that goes smoothly.

     Another problem. Ledges. So much of this game does not gel with me, because it is targeted towards Skate or Jet Set fans and players. So, when I pop off a trick, and smoothly stick the landing on top of, say, a wall, or a ledge, I would be rewarded. With the ability to keep going. This just snaps you into ragdoll, like you broke a core rule. It's one thing when you vault, that works fine. But doing a flip and landing on it? Heh, chill yourself kid. This ain't Skate. You'd think that wouldn't be a problem. It costs me time in trials. That's a big problem. Also, there are bumps and ledges you can barely see. Oh, you don't believe me? Sounds off? Plywood boards. These are solely an aesthetic thing. But have their own model. With collision. This stops your flow. So. Much. It feels out of nowhere, cruel and nasty, and I know it wasn't at all intended, because the thing's paper thin and has like, no use in platforming besides as like, a jumpoff marker. Which, uh . . . yeah, knowing what it can do, I'd prefer not.

     And then Trick Runs. The only challenges I can gold. And that's not a good thing. They can consist of racking up three simple tricks in a time limit to get a desired score. There is a barrier that shrinks around you. If you are outside that barrier, your tricks do not count. So what I do is stick unnaturally tight to start, and just go in tight circles doing the same three tricks. Now imagine doing this maybe up to three times for most levels. It is an uninspiring task of tedium that demands no learning curve out of you. Also, the friggin' plywood is right there on one of em'. Did they do that to mess with you? No. They just didn't test this enough. Or maybe at all.

     Would I recommend this? Not right now, no. For beginners, it's not forgiving. But that's mostly the level design. And so, for Skate veterans even, I also would not recommend this to. It needs fixing. Ledges shouldn't be run enders if you stick the landing. Ziplines need to be a thousand times more forgiving. Revoke collision on a solely decorative object. Better guide players on the runs, or just rework the base maps so it actually goes like someone ran through this, because it's almost no dice on certain ejects or wall runs. I cannot even foresee myself doing these uninterrupted. I can't get bronze. The lowest bar for trials. I- I looked at the tutorials, okay, I can do the moveset. I can rack things up. I know how to climb, and vault and I can do THOSE in succession. But this wall just won't work the way I want it to. This plywood board sent me flying higher than any of my jumps could. I won't grab the zipline, HOW 
 C O U L D  YOU GET A BEST TIME??? I'm dying, I can feel myself shrivel. And again. Trick Runs don't help me feel better. They are no fun. I can get why the achievement stats are like this. People are having fun. They're just not having fun playing the game part. Or . . . the challenges. I can get a badass run on my own, but tell me to go YOUR route and I just don't feel like I'm supposed to even be here.

     Just giving an honest review. I hope it does get better. I know there's only so much to expect . . .
But really, stop that crap with the plywood, I am 100% sure that shouldn't even be happening, and it has way too consistent stopping power. It's not even rarely. I am very acquainted with this splintery bastard in the 4 hours of me playing.

The developer of this game is a liar and a downright scammer. He made dozens of promises for the gameplay (ie. one long continuous map like you see in the skate games) that we have not and clearly never will see. There's only a few tricks to choose from, the gameplay is janky and unpolished, and so is the rest of the game. This game is fun for about a grand total of 2 hours, and then it's stale forever. Even with the updates.
