While the concept of SimPlayers is interesting, in practice they feel much more like traditional NPCs than actual simulated players. They don’t meaningfully quest or progress through content; they mostly just exist in the world and wander around. Because of this, the illusion of a living MMO breaks fairly quickly.
Class balance is another issue, especially when playing Duelist. As a Duelist, my damage feels significantly lower than that of SimPlayers, to the point where I often feel like the weakest member of my own group. SimPlayers consistently outperform the player in combat, which makes personal skill and class choice feel less impactful.
The combat itself also suffers from poor feedback. Hits often lack impact, animations and sound effects don’t clearly communicate successful strikes or effective ability use, and it’s difficult to tell when you’re playing well versus simply being carried by your party. This makes combat feel flat and unsatisfying, especially in longer fights.
These issues combine into a larger gameplay problem: the most effective way to play is simply to recruit strong SimPlayers and let them carry you. There’s very little incentive to rely on your own combat ability or decision-making when SimPlayers can do most of the work more efficiently. As a result, progression can feel passive rather than rewarding.
Lastly, the chat system hurts immersion more than it helps. Dialogue lines repeat very frequently and lack context or variation, making conversations feel shallow and artificial. Over time, this repetition becomes distracting and reinforces the feeling that SimPlayers are scripted NPCs rather than believable characters.
Overall, Erenshor has a compelling idea and atmosphere, but these issues make it difficult to stay immersed or feel like the player is truly at the center of the experience.

I really wanted to like it, but everything just feels so... sticky. Targeting mobs feels bad, starting attacks feels bad, movement feels bad, interfacing with the UI feels bad, everything just has a heavy coating of friction to get anything done. Even trying to trade and buy items feels bad.

I really like the idea behind the game though, but I would trade in a few of those 35+ zones for the UI / UX and movement experience to get more polish instead. I can't really recommend it in the modern gaming landscape, but I think it would have been a standout 10-15 years ago.

This is probably great for those that it's for, but it doesn't seem to be for me.

Notably, it's a "single player MMO" not in that it's designed with a lot of solo content and bots to replace groups for group content - instead, it's a "single-player MMO" in that you're expected to always be in a party with and managing a number of bots.

I really wanted to love this game, but between a Paladin that does zero damage and a quest/UI system that feels unfinished, playing it quickly becomes more frustrating than fun. I love old school MMORPGs and namely Asheron's Call is my favorite game of all time by a landslide, so I came into this very much wanting to enjoy it.

I made a Paladin thinking I'll be a 'hybrid' tank type character, that expectation was completely wrong. I literally do no DPS at all. So I'm reduced to just watching 5 other NPCs play the game and do all the damage while I sit there and effectively do nothing at all. Some kind of warning that the class has almost no damage would have been nice before investing my time leveling and getting off the island.

The UI and quest log leave a whole lot to be desired, they feel more like placeholders than finished systems. The quest log is literally just a blue rectangle with a bunch of plain text in it, offering little clarity or feedback. I can understand the concept of not holding your hand, but this game makes little to no effort to guide the player or communicate expectations in any meaningful way.

It doesn't need to be a fully polished 'modern' MMORPG, but a little more consideration for player feedback, readability, and class clarity would go a long way. There's a good idea here, but the execution makes it very hard to fully enjoy.

Also, receiving "clown" awards instead of counterarguments pretty much reinforces my points.

While the concept of SimPlayers is interesting, in practice they feel much more like traditional NPCs than actual simulated players. They don’t meaningfully quest or progress through content; they mostly just exist in the world and wander around. Because of this, the illusion of a living MMO breaks fairly quickly.
Class balance is another issue, especially when playing Duelist. As a Duelist, my damage feels significantly lower than that of SimPlayers, to the point where I often feel like the weakest member of my own group. SimPlayers consistently outperform the player in combat, which makes personal skill and class choice feel less impactful.
The combat itself also suffers from poor feedback. Hits often lack impact, animations and sound effects don’t clearly communicate successful strikes or effective ability use, and it’s difficult to tell when you’re playing well versus simply being carried by your party. This makes combat feel flat and unsatisfying, especially in longer fights.
These issues combine into a larger gameplay problem: the most effective way to play is simply to recruit strong SimPlayers and let them carry you. There’s very little incentive to rely on your own combat ability or decision-making when SimPlayers can do most of the work more efficiently. As a result, progression can feel passive rather than rewarding.
Lastly, the chat system hurts immersion more than it helps. Dialogue lines repeat very frequently and lack context or variation, making conversations feel shallow and artificial. Over time, this repetition becomes distracting and reinforces the feeling that SimPlayers are scripted NPCs rather than believable characters.
Overall, Erenshor has a compelling idea and atmosphere, but these issues make it difficult to stay immersed or feel like the player is truly at the center of the experience.

It's clear this game is doing something right. MMOs have an innate competitiveness that can make people feel left behind if they don't play often enough. This game essentially tries to offer the MMO experience without that shitty feeling. 

In actual MMOs, for the most part, you still have the option to solo some content. It's hard to do that in this game. In MMOs, typically overland content is designed for solo play in mind and a few challenges that necessitate group play, like Hogger in WoW. But in this game, everything is an "elite". The whole world is a dungeon that requires a group. "But Yogurt, there are workarounds! You can adjust the damage and hp of mobs in the main menu, or you can solo mobs a lower level than you!" Yes, and I agree, the decision to put those settings in by the dev was a good one. But the game isn't balanced around solo content. Let me explain. Mana costs and damage of spells are very specifically adjusted for group gameplay. And if you run out of mana, you usually die. What this means is that if you lower the global hp values too low, you oneshot everything and nothing is a challenge. When you don't lower it enough, you run out of mana before you kill anything and you die.

Even in a game like this, where it costs you nothing to recruit a bot for help, I still think the solo option is important. First of all, it varies the gameplay since you have to rely solely on yourself and your own character's skills and abilities. Second, it allows you to compare your strengths and weaknesses that you can supplement with party members if you choose. For example, when leveling a rogue in WoW classic, you're a glass cannon. You've got no healing, hardly any armor. Pulling more than 2 mobs can mean the end of you. But you hit like a truck. So by the time you can do dungeons, you're very aware of what your role is and develop an appreciation for classes that fill other roles.

In addition to a lack of reliable solo play, there is also a lack of other gameplay options that can keep players invested. There is no "pvp" in this game. You cannot duel the bots. There are no arenas, battlegrounds. This may sound silly, because they are bots after all, but I think it's important to at least be able to measure how you'll stand up against another class. And this will likely be never be implemented because of the odd balancing in the game. At level 10, I have about 360 hp. My strongest spell hits for over 200 damage, and I'm a druid. My party members frequently hit for 400 and 500. The game is simply not adjusted for any kind of pvp. It's more diablo-esque, where you have an irrevelant hp pool and hit ridiculous numbers on mobs with ridiculous health pools.

There's also no sort of talent tree, or anything to set you apart. You're just another duelist/druid/paladin etc. There are also only 5 classes, and parties are typically made up of 5 people. It's a party-based RPG and the only choice you can make is which of those 5 spots to fill. Also, despite there being fishing and mining, there's no skill associated with these, making grinding pointless unless there's a specific item you want. 

Here's the thing. It's hard for me not to compare this directly to my own personal private vanilla WoW server with bots, because I have that on my computer and have been playing it casually for the past 8 months or so. And on top of that, it's free, while this game costs $20 dollars. At least this game is easier to install by miles, I'll give it that. Of course this isn't a bad game, and if I had a sideways thumb, that's how I'd rate it. But once these issues clicked with me, it was hard for me to pick the game up again, and I'd exit out after a few minutes due to sheer boredom. The art style doesn't do it any favors either. In spite of these issues, if the game was pretty to look at, I'd probably love it. But it doesn't. It looks like a unity asset flop.

I want to leave a positive review so bad. The gameplay is good. Combat feels very comfortable. Questing feels just like it did back in the day of oldschool mmos.

My biggest gripe is the textures. They are so alarmingly annoying that its prevented me from having any enthusiasm to play any more of the game. Specifically the grass textures. Its one thing to use a crazy amount of moving particles when you are using high spec graphics and they blend together smoothly. However with the low spec style of this game, it does not mesh whatsoever. Every single blade of grass stands out almost more then my actual character does. As someone with vision problems, Its a complete nightmare to try and not strain my eyes playing this. Its like im playing a bullet hell game and instead of thousands of bullets on my screen are just blades of grass piercing into my eyes.

ill come back to this game at a later time to see if there is any adjustments to this. Like i said, everything else is wonderful. But until then i just cant get past this glaring issue.

The idea of a singleplayer MMO is cool but I just can't wrap my head around why anyone would play this instead of playing an actual MMO, it's all the boring parts without the fun parts that come from interacting with people, being part of a guild and stuff, all in all I just came out of this feeling like I wasted my time and did not have much fun doing it. Maybe if I had a podcast or something in the background that would have helped I suppose.

I want to like this game, but it's honestly lame. Seemingly glitched monsters with crazy defense or some kind of regen are in the mix and wipe you, this has kept me stuck in the tomb of the opening area. Yes I made groups and assigned roles. Hours in with Paladin and I only have Taunt, Kick, and a Heal. You can press C to consider monsters and it doesn't matter because it's so inconsistent. Jank movement, ALT-TABing changes my hotbar every damned time. The map controls are blocked by the UI. I'd try other classes but at this point I'm just annoyed I played past the refund time. Hope it gets better in 2026.

There's obviously been a lot of work put into it but this game is really clunky and cumbersome in every aspect. I'm 46 minutes in and I've had enough. 

Thinking I'll sit on this for a while, maybe check back in a year or two and see if things get any better.

I don't feel like I deal enough damage and I am required to party up with sim players for every quest, there isn't enough things to do, I ran around an entire zone to only find two quests that aren't even in the starting level range of where the game began. I want to like this game since I love classic rpgs but I'm gunna wait a while for this one. Just disappointed.

As a game the combat is bad, the animations are bad, the UI is bad and optimization is REALLY bad.
As a MMO the simplayers are dumb, they feel like robots that only know how to reply to like 10 things.

Local multiplayer for a game that has a lot of chaos in the later rounds makes for a difficult time for anyone trying to play with friends via remote play.  Really fun for the price, just won't really play with more than 1-2 people until they add servers sadly.  This could be such a great party game with 4 people with servers, it's a shame.

This is just awful. Even on sale, it's priced too high. This could be acceptable only if it's free to play.

It has exactly ONE mode, with basically no customisation. You can choose the two characters to make up the teams, that's it. It doesn't explain anything, there's no real tutorial, you're given a brief crash course hidden in a menu that doesn't help a whole lot. You can't adjust difficulty to make it easier to learn, either, so expect tremendous frustration from the very start.

You can also expect [i]extremely annoying gameplay[/i], like my first match, where halfway through, the opposing team did...something...and for the entire rest of the game, I couldn't see a full quarter of my screen because of some insane old woman screaming randomly around it. [i][b]It never went away.[/b][/i] I started to get a headache because while I do like the bright colours of the game, it just keeps flashing a bunch of obstacles onto your screen, without any rhyme or reason to it, and it gets really annoying very quickly. The magic effects your opponents have are also mostly just irritating and make it less fun to play, like turning you into a playing card or just cluttering your screen so you have an even harder time just moving around or even seeing your character.

The game is not well made, it's not coded very well, and it's not fun to play. You have no options if the difficulty is too high, which it is, especially as it's utterly unexplained outside of the bare minimum to even operate the game at all. It's not tight enough to do that.

Nothing about this game makes it worth having. Don't waste your money on this. If it were given more development and more actual content to it, it might be worth playing. As it is, this is something that should be playable for free to try and get interest for a full game, though I can't imagine it would give a remotely good impression or make anyone want to get anything more like this. Anime tie-in games should be more polished, and indie games should be more charming. This game is neither of those. You kind of want to like it for its different visual style, but this is the worst possible application of that style, because paired with the game, it just ends up being annoying beyond belief.

I hate to write a review this harsh but this game is absolutely terrible, not even worth being called a "videogame".

I love the arts and the flashy colors, but that is all this game has.
I'd like to say that this game is absolutely unbalanced, but the truth is that there is no concept of gameplay whatsoever. You just have to decide whether you want to pass or hit the ball and that's it. You don't even have to position yourself correctly: just press the button (not even timed right, mashing it works perfectly) and you'll hit the ball for sure with your character teleporting to the ball's position.
The animations easily start to become extremely messy and you won't even understand what the hell is going on on the screen. Just mash the button and randomly, sooner or later, either you'll score a point or your opponents will.
Also, in single player you cannot even switch between your two characters; you can only use the one you select first.

Really a huge disappointment. Looking at this game through a video will give you the exact same result of playing it: just seeing the cool characters and the animations.
It's true that the price is not very high, but I wouldn't feel like recommending it anyway unless you have friends to play it with and you're ready to spend MAXIMUM a few hours on it.

The camera of this game burned out my patience. I tried to overcome it, but it was just impossible.

[h3]Cool Stuff[/h3]
+ The game is pretty and the controls are actually pretty good.
+ If you play it in winter you can feel cozy at home seeing all those snowy mountains and stuff.

[h3]Not so Cool Stuff[/h3]
- THE CAMERA. You don't play this game thanks to it. You play it despite of it. When you're moving towards the screen it's all good, but if not, there will be more and more situations in which you'll need to know the course by heart, or you'll just be unable to react in time, because you don't see where you're going. Or maybe you will. Maybe I could have done it if I had drunk 20 coffees before starting playing, but that was not the plan. The camera, instead of looking ahead of the character, leaves you just enough space to leave fractions of a second to react to some obstacles, or won't even show you where you'll land after a jump, or where will you slide after entering a frozen water zone (where you can lose looots of time). And that just doesn't feel fair. Not in a game where each second counts to get rewards and progression.
-- Also, since the cam is so far, there will be times when the character will run over an unnoticeable bump that will make them jump just enough so you lose control while airborne, without even noticing, so you lose that already short reaction time you have to not crash.

-- And you could tell me: "But hey, there's a free camera you can switch to anytime! That should solve the issue right?" And I would answer: "Right! It SHOULD". But for some reason, that camera blurs whatever there is ~15m ahead, just so the game can make sure that you have no damn idea of where are you going with this camera either. Also, there's the extra perk that with the free camera you lose your sense of direction, so it's easy as hell getting out of the course.

-- Also, the free camera got bugged on me several times: when I tried to swap to it, it would remain still at the start of the course until I restarted from the beginning.

-- To make it even worse, I played the previous bike-themed Lonely Mountains game! And it was the same! (though the courses were more readable at least). Never more.

Despite not being a fun of the genre, I LOVED Lonely Mountains Downhill, it gained a very positive review from me.

A few minutes have been enough for me to refund this instead.
Nothing about it is even remotely as good as the old game, and it feels like a cheap copy-pasta.

The environments are ugly and have lost all their charm,
The movement feels weird and unsatisfying.
The physics of skying are just not as fun as the bike's.
The game's menus and general UI are hideous and unreadable.
The new behind-the-shoulders camera was a horrific idea.

A complete letdown that managed to repel me in a matter of minutes.

Brilliant game, as long the camera is behind you. Once the camera is in front of you, its full idiotic guessing where to go with no idea or indicator of the correct path. 

And, of course, you cant change the camera angle. 

The impressive nature, the great game play and the beautiful snow feeling, are all completely ruined by the fact above. Instead of racing through the wild nature, you have to learn by memory where to go. No fun at all. Refunded.

Game really hasn't improved in 4-5~ years since the last one. Players look awful, commentary is the same, every ball plays the same and only changes on it's hook rating (which are hilariously wrong on a lot of balls). Barely any new patterns added, no actual US Open or USBC Masters patterns for their literal events in career. No career development other than grinding through a map, higher tier balls mean next to nothing. Horrible pin action physics, many bugs that are prevalent in every match you bowl. 

The limited options throughout the entire game also show how little care is put into this game. It's all extremely surface level stuff you know bowling beyond a league level. You can't adjust layouts (because these balls don't have actual cores/cover stocks). Every professional plays the exact same as each other, i.e. LaVoie has the same rev rates as 2 handers which isn't even close to true. You can't even adjust a half board like they do in real life.

There's no ability to have a stock shot like you would in real life with a default rotation. You really can't adjust rev rates either with how their throwing system is. Speed adjustments are based off of a percentage and you're given no context as to what 100% would be.

Overall product is mediocre at best, but charging $40 for a game that's barely changed comparatively to 2021 is hilarious to me, wish I would've refunded while I could have.

As a bowler myself I was looking for different game, here are my comments:
*Positive: 
-Excellent pin & ball physics
-Nice real PBA player models and reactions
-Good pin sound effects
-Nice to have a career mode
-Good amount of customization of the player during career mode (Apparel & equipment)

*Not so nice:
-I was expecting more dynamic mechanics during the game (Approach, steps, aiming, swing, hook technique, perks and cons of 1 handed vs 2 handed player, etc) But the game if pretty boring, you really don´t control the "speed" of the ball or the swing. If anybody played "Skate" on console you probably know what I meant, using the right stick to control the swing & speed of the ball would have been a lot better than just selecting the speed and just pressing a button in a quick time event. 
-Spare shooting is pretty straight forward specially when you have single pins, I mean, you can´t just miss. Would have appreciated a more challenging gameplay to develop your own player and work both strikes & spares.
-Loved the physics of the ball & the pins, but I mean, you can just play straight and never miss the headpin.
-No difference between 1 handed & 2 handed players (Would have been nice to make the spare game more challenging for 2 handed and the hooking of the ball more challenging for 1 handed) but nahh it is the same.

*Overall:
It is the most realistic bowling game, but not the most entretaining. You will bet bored after a while, even if you are a hardcore bowler

I have had the game now for three days and I can tell you something. I know it is hard to hear, but it is not the best bowling game in the world. Here is why. 

Yes, the game has the official PBA license. Unfortunately it is outdated for the most part. As you can see from the trailer there are balls that are retired almost 15 years ago. And what comes to the brands of products, you are forced to use the Storm Bowling products for the 1st 2 tiers. I would rather use a no-name fantasy balls at 1st and then upgrade to the brand I want to use. Also, the game should have a sell ball feature to get rid of the balls that you don't use. 

The Player models are outdated. It looks like they were done in 2015 instead of 2025. Also some of the players have weird quirks with their faces. For example Chris Barnes looks more like Jim Carrey's impersonation of Clint Eastwood than himself and Jason Belmonte looked like he was dug up from a grave, not forget to mention Sean Rash, who's face looks more like a street bum. Most of the body types don't fit to their real-life versions. 

The next thing comes from the aspect of a PC player. This game is ported directly from the console version which shows up as the lack of proper mouse control. I mean, who came up with the idea of setting up the player with A&D, managing the angle with arrow keys, and then go back to set up the hook with A or D and just do the release with the mouse? This all could be done with just the mouse using the 3-click-system. The current system makes throwing very unrepeatable. Even though I have set the player to the same position and haven't touched to the the throw power and try to do the release as close as possible, with 4 shots I'll get 4 totally different dhots

If you play with just a game pad, the control system is similar to consoles. Also the graphic settings are very lacky of settings and still the game is not stable above 60 fps even with high-end PC. The player might move at 60 fps, but the rest of the animations are locked to 30 fps. This shows me that the game is still done mostly with the original mobile engine to be released to phones like the previous 21 and 23.

The pin Physics are decent, but they are very similar to what they were three years ago. The pins fly in the real way, but the floor is kind of sticky. 

The Career mode has new kinds of game modes which is a good thing. The league just makes them feel a bit wonky and arcadey, because the other players aren't wearing a matching team suits. It reminds more of SIMS bowling than a simulation that you take Seriously. 

With the current state and price, I cannot recommend this game for you, if you play on PC.
If you want to play an arcade bowling game, then buy this when the price drops. Otherwise Get something else, like the Premium Bowling.

Would really love to give this game a great review but as things currently stand i simply cannot. 
I understand that the game is made in Poland and the language is, by default, Polish. However.....at least give us a clue as to how to setup in English rather than wading through various different menu's in Polish trying to find out how to turn the default language to English. 
And then there is the Elephant in the room. The UK leagues are set up with no points limit and you can have any rider 1-6 as long as you have a junior at no7.  For anybody that enjoys the tactical side of speedway(as this is a management game, i presume most that buy) this is just silly and takes away any real management of forming a team under the points limit and using proper tacts during the match. Also, not sure when the tac sub limit kicks in, but it deffo didn't at 6 points down. 
Really want to love the game but it lives on a different planet to speedway in the UK.
If anybody needs to change to English it is the 3rd option on the list in the main menu and in the box that says Polski, click on that and you get a dropdown to change to English. 
Just as a suggestion to the devs, perhaps if you are going to launch the default game in Polish then some flags on the main screen would assist those that need to change language?

I will preface this review by saying I love IE and would love to give this a good review if I could but after completing the final match of story mode I felt the game was finally getting somewhere just to be met with the credits, I understand that from a production of this scale you will get more quality even if it is shorter but the length of the story is mostly artificial through forced grinding between matches. (My hours are 95% story mode with about 10 hours AFK and 5 hours looking for recruits so the story length for me was about 35 hours). 

The beginning of the game takes hours just for the the introduction of playing matches which would be fine if it made up for it in what is to come.

Another thing I looked forward to was recruiting players and the post game, while you can recruit there aren't many prospects that 1. out stat the base teammates 2. Are interesting in their own way. The majority of recruitable players just have a copy pasted personality type. After recruiting a single player that felt somewhat interesting to me and then seeing their stats not be up to par for what I want I thought I'll just save recruiting for post game as sidelining the base team for unrelated characters that aren't that good just didn't make sense so I reach post game and post game just doesn't exist there is one match you can play that doesn't have a connection the world that you play in you only enter it from the menus and I beat the match 21 levels underlevelled first try.

I can't really speak on competitive or chronicle mode but I will, For ranked I've not seen good things it just seems whoever has more hours logged has better stats so they automatically win because there is no tangible skill barrier in this game.

For chronicle mode It seems mixed many like the runback of historical matches and its progression and others aren't a fan of the mobile game like path of matches finding it too repetitive and tedious to have to fight every team twice if you want to recruit.

It seems like the game is trying to be too many things at once leaving each aspect unpolished.

If future updates can make a significant impact on my experience I would like to change my mind.

Singleplayer modes are fine, but online is a HORRIBLE experience; Cheaters, AFK players, the sweatiest of meta players and even when you do find a good match for you it just doesn't feel that satisfying. Considering this is a 70 euro game AT BASE, I'd want my experience with the game to be a lot better.

I've waited a long time for this game, bought the preorder twice, and I really wish there were an option to give a mixed review. I see a lot of reviews telling how amazing this game is but not talking about the problems that are here. If reviews were worse, I probably wouldn't leave this review, but as they are positive, I choose to leave it. I completed story mode, played some chronicle mode, and managed only through one competition route.
As for story mode, I liked it. I loved the characters; matches were fine most of the time—some were worse, some were better—but when I completed it, I felt disappointment because there wasn't much character development. It looked to me like the game was abruptly finished. Still, I liked it, and my negative review is not from story mode. If I could rate both separately, story mode would be positive. There were some frustrating things, like recruiting a random player for no reason that took me almost 2 hours, and overall recruitment to your team—even the one in story mode—I mean, they could at least give a different minigame for each set of players. But I don't want to spoil it; most of the people who played this game will probably understand my point here.
My negative review is from chronicle mode and some horrible design decisions by the Level-5 design team. Chronicle mode works like this: you have multiple competition roads. To go to the next match, you need to first win the story match, and secondly, you need to win this match with your team on the easiest difficulty.
So to progress, you need to play each match twice. Even if you are not interested in the players you are playing against and just want to go further, you still need to go through it twice without challenge because you can't select difficulty from the beginning. You want more challenge? You can play this match two more times on harder difficulties. I felt that the game does not respect my time at all. Play a match, do some objectives, play a second match, get 5 or more goals because it's no challenge at all, go next. I won't even start talking about the economy in chronicle mode because it's a joke and it's not balanced for the player at all.

The Strikers game I was playing, for example the 2013 version, had a place for your football club to recruit players there and to train. There is nothing like this in chronicle mode—only matches, and it gets repetitive pretty fast. Play a match, play it again, score 4-5 goals, go next. There was some cool stuff in story mode that could be reimplemented into chronicle mode, like changing audio during matches and training mini-games, but it felt like there were only matches and UI.
Bugs—yes, there were multiple. Once I blocked myself in the interface in story mode, saved from a different panel view, and had to reset the interface because it got unresponsive, probably because of the state of it and it didn't know if it was supposed to read cursor or button inputs. Teleporting castle walls, I got some crashes too, and it really sucked when they happened during a match in the second half. It wouldn't be that bad if the game hadn't been delayed so much, so a working interface is not that big of an expectation, especially with that price. Audio? Mostly works great, but sometimes in my matches, audio changed multiple times without reason at all every 2 minutes, and sometimes I had to restart the game because it started to make a weird noise.
So is this game good? Yes, but I don't think it deserves the reviews it got. There are multiple problems that need to be addressed and some that could be addressed. With that price, I expected a product that I would sink at least 100 hours into, but it only kept me interested for the duration of story mode. I will probably play more after some time, or maybe when modders fix the issues I've written about here, but my opinion is that if you are interested in this game, it's better to wait for a discount because it's not worth the price it's listed for.

It seriously hurts giving this game a negative rating with 140 hours of playtime, but it is what it is.

Chronicle Mode? A sloppy grind fest. I could go into it with 50 levels less than the CPU and still sweep because the AI is actually stupid.

Competition / Online?
The second you get to the golden league, quit it. The story could’ve leaned more on tactical thinking from the coach, like Blue Lock does with Isagi. Instead, it falls into the “coach gives vague orders, then explains them after it works” trope that the original games already did a lot. Side characters don’t mesh with the main cast much, making me wonder if sticking to 11 dedicated players might have streamlined things, though the current approach probably allows for more story depth.

Even after having no equipment and not using any beans I was way too overleveled during practice games. If you just let us choose which level we want to play the practice game at this would have been fixed. Really unfortunate because the games are extremely boring You don't even have to do anything to win basically it just wastes a lot of time.

The RPS combat started off really good but after 3-5 battles you barely have to try anymore to win I remember just pressing the same button over and over again to win by default.

The main villain is probably the weakest in any Inazuma Eleven game. He  basically barely got started and immediately folded when he got confronted. His involvement in the story is practically zero everything would have unfolded the exact same had he not been in it.

Gameplay feels less responsive and accurate than the ds games somehow. You can never choose who to pass to the cursor just does what it wants. The attack command just makes all characters move in random directions and passing during that time becomes even messier. The cursor when aiming for a shot gets stuck on corners of the goal occasionally making you miss whatever chain shot you tried to set up. Sliding tackles are way too overpowered so if you are going online it's all you will do. Defending just doesn't work at all. you can't control where your defenders go and they don't move fast enough so they are useless unless they happen to be directly blocking the course already.

This is gonna sound silly, but the Roblox Inazuma Strikers game has better, more accurate controls. Having story matches being at the same level (but with way lower stats for some reason, lvl 70 gk with only 1100 hps?) is not enough to compensate literally everything else not being upscaled. As a long time Inazuma Eleven fan that holds the series and games close to my heart, Victory Road has been a massive disappointment for me. While I really like the new characters, every part of this game feels massively undercooked, with Story mode lacking any form of endgame content to make it worth playing after finishing the final match and Chronicle mode being a boring slog that gives very short sparknotes versions of matches I have already seen executed better in their respective games.

In addition, while this game does feature every character in the series, those characters are recruited via a gatcha system, which makes building specific teams an incredibly dull experience, as you both have to grind online modes to get the currency to pull for characters, but you then have to rely on luck to pull eleven specific players, which has completely killed my investment in building the scout character teams I had planned out for each part of chronicle mode.

Finally, my most personal issue with Victory Road is due to its English dub, which is both low quality and half finished (The entirety of chronicle mode is voiced in Japanese, but none of the matches are in english. The RPG style battles have a similar issue). They also decided to give the characters an American localisation, which is a shame to see as historically every game in the series has had a very charming and fairly competent British localisation that was a large reason for me to connect with the series in the first place.

It breaks my heart, but I cannot recommend this game in its current state. Hopefully lessons can be learned for the next one, but I think the problems with this game are not ones that can be easily fixed with updates unless Level 5 rewrites and restructures both story and chronicle mode.

(Summary at the end of the review)
I waited for this game for a long time, and I don’t really know if it was worth it.
The first thing that I HAVE to say and, a lot have already said is that this game is full of bugs (it’s not worth it to list all of them because there are too many, some are being fixed, some are popping up randomly after the patches, it’s literally a mess) , online games at launch were barely playable, servers suck, and so on all …but it is hard to admit that this is not the bad part.
•	Gameplay (I mean the matches’ gameplay, not the overworld gameplay, this one is normal: the player runs around talking and doing quests, pretty fine). The BIG part that is really bad (obviously in my opinion, all of this review might be outrageous to someone, but this is how I see the game) are focus battles. Focus battles are the most ambiguous system of dribbling: 
In old mainline games two players clash, they chose to use or not a technique, the highest value wins. Simple and straightforward
In Strikers all the gameplay is in real time, when players clash the more skilled one wins the ball, or you can choose to use slides to secure the ball with a higher chance of committing a foul, you can use techniques. Simpler and more straightforward
In this game when a focus battle starts from the defender pop out “hands” which length is determined by his defense stats (compared to the attacker attack stats) and the attacker has to pass through them, BUT: if the defender’s stats is higher than the attacker’s, the latter can’t pass through the hands but if it is the opposite than he can dash through them, if the attacker dashes into defender’s hitbox than he SHOULD be stopped, BUT sometimes it doesn't happen because maybe there is some RNG behind and my whole explanation might be useless, if you are playing online it is even more random because netcode sucks, special techniques don’t start even if you smash the buttons…
Did you understand? No? Don’t worry, it is very complicated and the game doesn’t bother to explain any of this, so it is hard to understand even for pro players.
Other than gameplay AI sucks: it is not unusual to find defenders in the zone (near the opponent goal), when there is a long shot the players who should block the ball don’t go towards the ball trajectory to try to block it, which is something that happened in all previous Inazuma games (and we can’t do anything about it)
Opponent AI is even worse, so bad that Level 5 had to do a patch that made matches finish in the first half if you score 4 or more goals, because matches are boring to play (I repeat myself, first time in Inazuma franchise, in previous games you could play how many times you wanted the same match and never get bored because there was actually a challenge)
When you pass the ball sometimes it goes offside, to the enemy goalkeeper, outside the court, but if you try to use the manual pass (so the X and Y buttons instead of the A button in the XBOX controller), the ball might go too close to another player so the cursor get locked to that one instead the one you targeted and you can’t do nothing about it. To clarify let’s do an example: left defender does a long pass to the left forward near the opposite goal, you manage to do it but in the process the ball passes too much close to the left midfielder, so the player is locked to that midfielder, you can’t switch player, the left forward stays still, the left midfielder runs all the way to the ball because you are controlling that player and you can’t switch or move it…and you wasted an opportunity to make a goal because the opponent managed to take the ball and drink a coffee in the meantime. I know it is a long and complicated explanation, but the game itself is unnecessarily complicated.
•	Story mode 
From what we saw in the trailer, the story was appealing because the main character hated football but even before the end of chapter 1 you discover that he loves it and it is again the same story, but it’s somewhat worse. The first half of the game is really long, and it is not a bad fact…if the story was worth it: there isn’t even anything wrong with ANOTHER main character who loves football but it’s nothing new. The protagonist recruit random members, mostly all of the character in the game have 0 development or even characterization (only Destin, Briar, Harper and a very little bit of Cade Shelby), you HAVE to recruit some scout players by doing a 2.30 minutes long minigame for 15 times, who, for coding reasons, won’t have impact on the story and will have minimal interactions with main characters (in the final cutscene you can even see scout players who you never recruited and so shouldn’t be part of your team)
The second part of the game is all matches, near to 0 story, the “Cedric in Shibuya” narrative arc was cut off the story so a Kenshin just spawn from him after some flashbacks and emotional slideshow.
So the story is a NO. Things happen just because, 0 development, trivial main story.
•	Extended story
Literally one chapter (at the moment) that consists in a match and a cutscene: solaria-zeus fights you, solaria-zeus’ captain transforms int Byron Love (PLOT TWIST), solaria-zeus lose, solaria-zeus friend, 0 impact on the plot
Embarrassing to be called story
•	Chronicle mode: too many matches, recycled or lazy animations. For example Bellatrix attacks Shiller with the same animation of previous 3DS games normal shots and then there is a hard-to-explain sort of slideshow of the 3d model of Shiller who becomes larger for 3 times, which was intended to be the ball’s pov which was going to hit him. Another example is the Ultimate Thunder tactics in GO saga is animated with 5 or 6 animations of normal shots (always from the previous games); the same animation in the same spot of the court by different characters.
In other words, no new animations (that I expected after approximately 10 years after the other games), a lazy work.
•	Other general information 
This game could be much better if there weren’t players passives, that force you to reroll and waste days if you want players slightly above average. In my perspective making Inazuma more gacha than it already was doesn’t extend its longevity, it makes it a lot less funny. But it’s 2025, make it gacha, people love Genshin Impact, don’t they? (No). 
I repeat, netcode sucks
The equipment shops is terribly managed: there are too many shops that sell too random things, with random placement (for example you beat a team and you unlock random shoes in the middle of the chronicle shop, then you beat the team after and you unlock an accessory at the very end of the competition shop). The same goes for story mode. Previous games managed better the shops
Every piece of equipment raises two random stats so it’s much harder to know what equip to whom. (Needless to say, that in previous games it was a lot simpler: Shoes increased kick and speed, Gloves increased catch, bracelets increased resistance, and so on)
Stats are counterintuitive and overcomplicated. Again, previous games (even Strikers) were better.
There is an embarrassingly low number of new techniques, most of them are recycled animations (at this point what didn’t they recycle?), they even recycled a new technique created in this game to make the same one with 15 more base power: meteoric fire tornado -> meteoric fire tornado V, the first (as long as I recall) Kenshin technique to not have a Kenshin in its animation (they did a similar thing when they carried majin the hand and bakunetsu storm from Inazuma eleven 3 to Inazuma eleven Go but at least they have the corresponding Kenshin in the animation)

Summary: strong nostalgia effect, too little new good content, a lot of trash, you may not like it if you are a critical player, if not still think twice before spending 70€.
Still i'll keep playing because i'm a fan.
I hope for the next game to not be a mix of a recycle bin and a slot machine.

[b]Gameplay[/b]
The gameplay is very responsive, the formations feel like they matter, there's a ridiculous amount of character customization and there's a ridiculous amount of characters. All of this lands while the game weans you on it's mechanics such that a total newcomer like myself could eventually get into the swing of things and start making my own decisions about team building and tactics. 

[b]Music[/b]
The game has a soundtrack that's not afraid to be heard and enjoyed. The music does an extremely good job at helping set the mood and a lot of the game moments are elevated by the sweet ambrosia gracing your eardrumbs.

[b]Story[/b]
The story has a lot of heart. You get to see characters go through arcs, contend with feelings, express passions about things they love. Things get dramatic, stakes are raised, goals are met, and the character emotions just hit home. 

Overall the game feels like something I would love as a kid, engendering in me those same emotions I would have had as a kid as an adult. If you like fun games and are a fan of sports games, buy this game.

Edit: 12/16/05
We're flipping this review. This game has one of the most horrendous grinds I've ever experienced in any game I have [b]ever[/b] played.

First off, let me begin with the issues.

[b]The passive problem[/b]
The game has units with variable passives inside a variety of archetypes and those passives have variable values. So to get a hero with passives that are [b]decent[/b] not perfect [b]decent[/b] it is a hell of pulling characters and dismissing them. Except there's no multi-summon. You have to do it individually. And there's no multi-dismiss. You have to do it individually. And it has the funny UI issue where everything takes longer than it needs to for no discernable reason.

[b]The UI[/b]
Continuing with these complains, the UI is bad. Let's say you want to search for coaches/managers. There's no option to sort for them. There are a variety of archetypes and they're [b]important[/b] to team building. There's no option to sort for those either. Equip a skill to a character and then forget which one has it? Good luck, chump. No options to scale by values of passive. No number sorting at all. This game has a player bank size of the thousands.

[b]The Challenge[/b]
The AI in this game is both atrociously stupid and horrifically unbalanced. RE-Story, the big feature they advertised for this update to let you fight against a story mode that's scaled up to you was lazily done. The opposing teams have the same exact moveset, and worse, they didn't properly scale their stats. So even with a team unequipped in everything you vastly outstat the dumb computer.

This is even worse in chronicle mode. 230 matches against some of the most inept AI known to mankind. They clearly chose quantity over quality. 


[b]Power Progression[/b]
As mentioned above, carefully selecting a team is a miserably painful experience. But what hurts this is the odds. The game has two ways to get heroes (the second highest rarity). Randomly get them at a 0.005% chance in a focus battle in chronicle mode or pull them from the gacha at a 0.05% chance (0.015% if you want a particular hero). There are no safeguards. There's no pity system or other gacha safety nets and pull results are fixed on account creation.

So actually getting a more powerful character is an RNG hellscape meant to waste your time.
But you'll notice I said [b]second[/b] highest rarity.

I want you to look at my hours spent and use this as a frame of reference when I say that I did not even come [b]close[/b] to getting the highest rarity. 

[b]Various currencies[/b]
Go do several hundred matches of this mode for currencies 1-5. Then do several hundred matches of this mode for currencies 6-9. Then do several hundred matches of this mode for currences 10-15. If you want currencies 16 and 17 go play online. You can also do chronicle for a [b]chance[/b] at the gacha currency or you can play and this terribly unbalanced online for a guarantee 5 of the gacha currency.

[b]Online balance[/b]
This is closer to the balance of an early 2000s fighting game in that the balance is so comically out of whack that not falling into meta gives you a 1:9 matchup against teams that do. That's assuming the match doesn't disconnect, the opponent doesn't forfeit on your first score, that you're not up against an obvious cheater with 4 of the highest rarity on their bench, etc etc.


If you're going to play this game. Just do the story and leave with the good memories.
There is absolutely no way anybody on the development team plays this post-game.

ABSOLUTE DOG***T

You know you're in for a bad time when you go to refund but realize that you're a little over two hours in, so you can't anymore. I decided to stick with it and hope the game gets better later since the story mode has no soccer matches until the 6-hour mark (tutorial didn't even begin until around 4 hours in). Unfortunately, while I was toughing it out through the slog of fetch quests and literal rock-paper-scissors "combat", the actual soccer in this game is a miserable experience. 

This game is nothing like other sports games. In this game, you will be stuck in a "focus battle" every second of dribbling the ball down the field or after every pass you make. The focus battle is more or less another rock-paper-scissors game with an element check, as well as a stats check. So unless the stats are very close for both teams, you will either win every focus battle or lose every single one. On top of that, everyone has to play an anime cutscene for every focus battle and every shot they take, and you can't skip them, so you get to enjoy the same 5-second cutscene hundreds of times!

Taking a shot at the goal is essentially the same thing. Instead of trying to hit the ball around the keeper to score a goal, your goal is to use an anime move so that you fire the ball 100 mph at the keeper, knocking him back and probably breaking his rib cage.

With the way the game is designed, you will easily win every single match in singleplayer. I was really excited for Inazuma Eleven Victory Road, and while the game does have real strengths, several design decisions seriously hurt the experience, especially if you're a fan of the GO, Chrono Stone, or Galaxy series.

Positive points

Very strong fidelity to the universe and atmosphere of the series, with well-placed music and overall good presentation.

Graphics, animations, and visual effects are impressive for an Inazuma Eleven title.

The story mode is a real treat.

Good strategic potential in team building and technique management.

Negative points and constructive criticism
1. Heroic difficulty in Chronicle Mode: too punishing since the latest patches

Since the recent updates, the AI practically has infinite tension, making some matches unfair and frustrating. I’m getting destroyed by Inazuma KFT while using a fully leveled, previously dominant team.

The AI should have a normal tension gauge like the player, progressing with time instead of acting in "cheat mode". Otherwise, you will push players to look for exploits or cheats.

The challenge no longer comes from strategy, but from artificial advantages.
A serious rebalancing is needed to make Heroic mode interesting rather than frustrating.

If you want extreme difficulty, create a secret path / ultimate route like in the older games, with max-level teams and late-game challenges.

2. Mix Max and Keishin: a clear downgrade

This is probably the most disappointing aspect for fans of GO, Chrono Stone, and Galaxy.

In the anime (and sometimes in previous games), we saw:

Entire teams using Mix Max (Zanark’s team), even if in the games only the AI could do it.

Players using Keishin and Mix Max together (Arion, JP, Zanark, etc.).

Full team transformations consistent with the lore.

Smooth transitions between Keishin and Armed Keishin.

In Victory Road:

Only two transformations can be active at the same time.

Impossible to switch smoothly from Keishin to Armed Keishin.

Impossible to teach Armed Keishin to a player who already has a regular Keishin.

Forced choice between either Keishin or Armed Keishin.

Transformation duration is extremely short.

These restrictions feel like a major regression compared to previous games and remove a lot of the fun.

3. Impossible to recreate iconic scenes from the anime

In Inazuma Eleven GO, several scenes show the entire team activating their Keishin at the same time. These were memorable and powerful moments that captured the spirit of the series.

In Victory Road, this is simply impossible.
The limit of two simultaneous transformations completely prevents recreating these iconic scenes, which is disappointing for a game intended to celebrate the franchise.

4. Technique progression is too rigid

Having to obtain the same technique multiple times just to level it up makes no sense, neither for the lore nor for gameplay.

Older games offered much more flexibility and were simply more enjoyable in this regard.
A freer, more intuitive system would be a huge improvement.

5. Meta too focused on Heroes

The meta revolves almost entirely around a small group of Hero players, which heavily reduces diversity online.
You keep running into the same team compositions over and over, making matches repetitive.

Heroes are a false good idea: out of thousands of players, you always end up fighting the same dozen characters.
A better balance would make PvP more varied and interesting.

6. Keishin art direction (personal opinion)

This is subjective, but I don’t really like the visual design of Keishin in this game.
It’s not a major issue, but combined with the heavy restrictions on the system, the result looks much less impressive than in the 3DS titles.

Conclusion

Inazuma Eleven Victory Road has a lot of potential and a solid technical foundation.
However, the unfair difficulty, the severe limitations on transformations, the rigid technique progression, and the inability to recreate iconic moments from the series make it feel like a step backward compared to previous games.

With more freedom, better balance, and a system closer to what was established in GO, Chrono Stone, and Galaxy, the game could reach an excellent level.
As it stands, it is good, but too restricted to truly satisfy long-time fans.

Things you should know about this game:

1. It's not about football, it's about a hallucinating boy who speaks with imaginary friend and kicks imaginary balls. 
2. The main sport here is rock-paper-scissors. 
3. It's not a game but an interactive anime where you play football matches sometimes.
4. There is not football matches actually because the football here is anime-football like a basketball in  «Kuroko no Basket» — everyone just uses superpowers. So, it's not FC.
5. Fat guys can be very fast.
6. Boys here play with girls on the same field.

For the record, I have not ever watched Inazuma Eleven. I am a fan of anime, but I am not really a fan of football, but the concept of a tactical RPG which makes football enjoyable really appealed to me. I was also sold on the overwhelmingly positive reviews on this game. 

With that, I really enjoyed the first two hours of the game. It is a very slow burn and I found the story really entertaining. I liked the characters, the build-up, and I was sold on the style and the graphics. The football is actually really fun and over-the-top. 

After 8 hours, I tried and failed to refund it. One of the reasons was due to an audio glitch which I have managed to fix, but I have read others with similar issues so that might need some fiddling if you decide to buy. The other reason is because:

In eight hours of gameplay, I played four games of football. Four games. The games were about 2 minutes in length each. The final straw for me was after getting to a point in the narrative where I was doing football try-outs to put together my team (again, 8 hours) - I was presented with three teams of six or so characters. Each character in the team had a somewhat unique personality. 

Now, the game asked me to test them out! So, I click on a team and you are invited to do a mini-game with one of them. It's a very tedious mini-game where they are in a sort of tussle for the ball with one of the main characters and then they point to one of three spots on the screen, you click the spot they indicated, and they catch the ball. They catch the ball and then a health-bar goes down. Once the health-bar reaches zero, you win the mini-game.

It's really, really boring. 

Now, do that six times in a row - one for each of the members of each try-out football team. As I was sitting there watching YouTube videos I had this sudden realisation of "oh my god is this game going to make me do this three times over? they cannot possibly be about to make me do this mini-game 18 times?" 

I want to be really clear. I was invested in the story, I liked the characters, I watched all of the cutscenes - I am not someone who was skipping through narrative to just get to the football. I finished the first 6 mini-games of this cluster and was presented with 12 more grinning NPCs waiting for their turn to play this shit mini-game and I closed the game down. 

8 hours was also the point in the story where I also stopped doing side-quests. Side quests will usually go like this "hello I am an NPC, can you escort me to the shops?" You will then walk to the shops with the NPC in your party and get some jellybeans. Sometimes, the NPC will say "hello I am an NPC, can we play rock paper scissors?" and you will play rock paper scissors with them. Rock paper scissors is really, really boring. 

Overall, if you are thinking of buying this game be aware that the game delights in making you do tedious mini-games for approximately an entire workday before it allows you to touch a football.

It is INSANE that people are saying the story or the characters are good or charming at all.  The character writing is absolutely godawful, the dialogue is atrocious, and the voice acting hurts both of the previous things so greatly that even if they had an iota of quality they would marked down severely from whatever emotionless NPC of a VA did these lines.  You play as a kid with some of the ugliest hair this side of Dr. Stone.  His hair looks like a green octopus, and he fancies himself as some kind of Social Architect who knows everyone's true motives, creates plan after deus ex machina plan that grasps the breadth of each new crisis like he's Light Yagami if Light Yagami was written by a sixth grader.  He is never wrong, or surprised, or challenged in any significant way.  He lacks any flaw except that his hair is ugly and he can't play soccer because of a heart condition.  I supposed the flat, single-take voice acting really sells the idea of him being a sociopath who doesn't function on the same plane as other humans if that's the angle they're going for, but I don't think so considering everyone else is just as flat personality and VA wise.  Each of the other one-dimensional main characters have their Generic Anime Protagonist Group role that you can surmise from their appearance alone and be completely correct.  You have:
-Gruff Guy With No Eyebrows
-Plucky Fun-loving Kid Who Walks Around With His Hands Behind His Head Because He Is SO Casual
-Girl Who Everyone Likes And Is A Popular Japanese Idol
-Cowardly Fat Kid
and
-Persnickety Kid on the School Council

The actual story gameplay is also bad and deeply inconsiderate to the player.  Why does it take hours because you play a singular game of soccer?  Why are you playing Rock Paper Scissors against townspeople in an incredibly unfun and easy to win minigame constantly?  Why when you do some kind of soccer training game to raise your stats it actually have nothing to do with teaching the player how to play the soccer part of the game?  And why does those same training minigames not tell you anything about how to do them?  It isn't difficult to figure the minigames out, but like, I'd rather they be much more difficult to do and be engaging but told how to do them, than just thrown some minigame with zero directions while spending time figuring it out then be uninterested anytime I do it again.  It is bad game design, the minigames should have to do with The Game (of playing a soccer rpg) when The Game (Inazuna Eleven) is focused around playing The Game (of playing a soccer rpg).  It's phenomenally short sighted.  Every one of those minigames could be removed and no one would be any better or worse at playing soccer.

The Story! The actual Story in Story Mode is boring as hell.  Maybe it's because the characters are so boorishly flat, but I can't imagine anyone was really invested in restoring the soccer club for any reason other than because they wanted to play soccer.  Perhaps the developers could have simply skipped like 8 gameplay hours of getting the soccer club up and running by having you go to a new school where the soccer club is already up and running.

Just starting another paragraph here to complain again about how dreadful the voice acting is.  If you were directing the VA on this game I hope you consider telling your voice actors to try acting, that would be my first recommendation.

I do not rec this to RPG players looking for a good single player RPG that will etch story beats and memorable characters into their brain.

I do rec this to RPG players looking for a sports RPG where they can min-max stats and skills to beat ass.

Edit: After playing this game more, I don't even think the gameplay is good.

Opponent players on the field are so close to you that there is very minimum amount of control you get over your characters before you enter one of the rpg battles, and I'm fine with rpg battles, that's what I wanted even, but the rpg battles themselves are depthless and repetitive. 

Scoring is incredibly lazy and poorly thought out.  Your shoots do x amount of damage the goalie has an HP bar that slowly regenerates, and as long as the goalie still has HP left after your shot it will not go it.  Keep in mind that the goalies HP is usually multiple times that of your shots, so its more about shooting often to wear them down before their Defense HP regens enough that you can't get another in.  You can shoot a shot in real time instead of as a special attack cutscene, but it's clunky and hard to aim in a way that it doesn't really feel like they intended for that to be part of the game.  But maybe that's how you're supposed to score and the giant Special Attack Cutscene Nonsense where you shoot a combination attack of lightning and fire from two characters or whatever is actually just bait and you shouldn't use it.

The "Tactics" as the game calls it are giant swathes of negative effects that cover most of the field and do things like turn off you having options, which if you think about it means the "Tactics" in fact are just the removal of the player having access to Tactical decision making.  It's really dumb and not satisfying on either end.

It's fun to play and talk shit about with your friends at least. But I'd absolutely get my money back if I could.

[h2] This game is nothing like what I was expecting [/h2]

I've been a long time fan of Inazuma Eleven and, like a lot of other fans, I was very excited for this game. All the trailers hyped it up like crazy as the ultimate Inazuma Eleven Experience. Sadly... I don't think it is.

[u] This review is based on my experience with content from the demo that made its way to the final game and the game's Chronicle and Online modes. [/u]

[b] The Positives: [/b]
The game looks gorgeous. The attention to graphics and anime cutscenes is outstanding and really makes this feel like a modern game. The ambition of bringing over 5000 players from across every game in the series as well as every previous mechanic (Keshins, Armourfy, Totems, etc) really adds tons of customization to the game. Also, this shows how much passion the developers had in really making this game feel like a celebration of the series.

[b] The Negatives: [/b]
Unfortunately, several design choices bring the whole experience down (and this not even including the myriad of visual bugs and translation errors that are sprinkled a bit all over).

[u] 1. Menus & UI: [/u]
The design of the menus is bad, they feel bloated with just too many options at once to navigate through creating a feelinng of clutter. Also, they make too much use of an oversaturated blue hue that makes them tiring to look at.

[u] 2. "Gachification" of character acquisition: [/u]
I put the first word in commas because the game is respectful enough to not have any sort of paid microtransactions for the characters. However, by adding rarity levels, it makes getting the characters you really love feel both cheap and a slog. Instead of "oh cool I finally got Fey Rune" it's more like "Oh I got a spirit of Fey Rune that now I have to spend time ranking up it's rarity by using the spirits of generic characters" (it makes your character feel incomplete and it makes you not care at all about generic characters which I get it, they're generic, but it's worse in this game than in previous titles of the series). 

[u] 3. Online Mode Issues: [/u]
Related to the previous point, the online mode rewards you with a lot of player spirits as well as in-game currency in a way that incentivizes players to, during a lot of online matches, either just focus on farming spirits through Focus Battles or just going AFK for most of the match [b] (And this is in ranked, mind you) [/b]

[u] 4. Lack of English Voiceovers in Chronicle Mode (for fans of the dub): [/u]
My final complaint, and granted maybe the most subjective one, is that the game fails to live up to its name for fans that grew up with the english dub. Inazuma Eleven was always known for it's teenage shonen action packed awesomeness, so, in a game of this scale meant to be a celebration of the series, how the hell can some super techniques (AKA hissatsus) not be voiced?? They did it in the Wii spin-offs of the series so why not here?? It felt so weird to open Chronicle Mode, play the match against Royal Academy, and then watch Mark Evans do his legendary God Hand in all its HD glory only for no sound to come out of his mouth. They didn't need to do this for every move on every character but at least iconic moves on iconic characters should have received this treatment!!! [u] Apparently, the Japanese dub has them... why not us? [/u] 

[b] Conclusion: [/b]
Overall, as strange as it sounds, I would rather recommend this game to beginners of the series who want to dip their feet into the crazy world of JRPG football rather than veterans of the series who I think will be greatly disappointed after the initial "honeymoon phase" of this game.

I'm sure the Story Mode has a fun and energetic story like previous titles but that alone can't carry this game and its 70€ price tag. I really wanted to support Level-5 but I'll be refunding this game.

I feel like there's a real need for a fun VR climbing game for PCVR, and was hopeful this might be it, or at least the start of it. Sadly, for now anyway, it isn't. I love the idea, and hope development keeps going and I eventually have to rewrite this review, but for now there are too many frustrations with this game for it to be something I could recommend.

Issues I felt it had:

- Graphically it's kind of mushy; I feel it could look more impressive than it does.
- The grip mechanic is really random and makes no sense whatsoever. Sometimes it grips, other times not. There is no indication of what makes for a working grip (yes, I get the lights on the glove, but when and why does it go red?)
- Movement when you're stood free has to be one of the worst implementations I've experienced in a modern VR game. There's no turn. Moving forward will randomly walk you backwards. 
- On the randomly walking backwards thing: when stood on a flat surface of a building, attempting to move to the wall to carry on can result in you being thrown off and killed for no obvious reason (because of the random backward walk).
- When I made it to the top of the first building, nothing happened. There was no indication of what the end-game situation was.
- This might just be a me thing, but also I found there was pretty much zero immersion in the game. Something like The Climb 2 *really* made me feel like I was high above the ground and in danger; there was zero sense of that with this.
- The music that plays when you're climbing is annoying, far too loud, and can't be turned off. I suspect this might be the immersion-killer now I think about it. If there was the sound of traffic and people below, sounds of the environment around me, the sound of wind blowing past me, I can imagine I'd feel far more immersed.

All in all I spent half an hour trying to make it past the very first level and the mechanics of the game seemed to make it impossible at every step. As I say, even when I made it to the top despite those issues, there didn't seem to be a way to actually finish the level.

You'd expect the first level to be almost impossible to fail as it will *teach* you the mechanics of the game.

I really want to like this. I really would love to see a fun climbing game. Right now, this isn't it. Right now I'd recommend waiting to see how it develops.

---

Post update update: I really appreciate the developer responding to what I wrote, and this gives me a lot of hope for this game; I've tried the update and, sadly, I can't honestly change this to a "recommend" at the moment.

The sounds are better, and it's great to have the music removed from the climb itself, but other than that all of the main issues remain:

- The grip system seems to be a random mystery with no skill needed at all, from what I can tell.
- Movement didn't appear to change at all, the way the thumb sticks work seems to ignore any of today's conventions for VR games.
- Walking forward and stopping still causes random jump backs, which still throws you off buildings if you're stood on a ledge.
- I did finally manage to unlock the second level, and made it to the very top, but the game never registered that I'd made it and it wasn't at all obvious what I was supposed to do to register that I was done.

This is the kind of climbing experience I was waiting on but it has many issues that could be fixed. Falling through structures sometimes, some parts of structures not being grabable. Id like to be able to roam the top when I finish a map and you sort of can but you just clip through the surface except for your head. Would also be cool to have the ability to throw yourself upward when climbing to try and climb higher faster. Overall its some fun and decent views. Came back to downvote because I finally made it to the Eiffel Tower and everytime I load in it spawns me behind an invisble wall I cant get around and wont let me climb it.

VR Sailing is good, this one is not good. I have not the feeling to climb. While climbing, visuals are junky, stuttering. You have the impression you're staying still and just moving the palace or monument around you! I know that's what happening in the game engine, the game world is doing inverse translation. But the game needs different mechanics to fool perception; translation should be edited such to give perception of realistic movements and climbing. In real life when I grab and climb with hands I have not the impression to translate the palace, my real life visuals while climbing are very different, I don't see the palace moving so fast and stuttering! Graphics are not good, textures are ugly. We need PCVR graphics: high res textures, volumetric effects, shadows, etc, please. Lot of collision bugs, e.g. you can fall inside the pyramid.

A fun climbing game with a couple of shortcomings:
- Quite often my Vive Wand crashes in this game (not in other games), e.g. the position is not correct anymore, or it walks a different direction than what I press.
- Instructions are difficult to read (too large projection, can't read it all) and (possibly because of that) incomplete (what does the red light mean? what do the colored blocks mean on the bottom left mean?)
- I would like to experience what it feels like to jump off, but the gravity is too low to truly experience that (the acceleration feels too little, compared to when I jump off a high bridge in real life).
- When you come on top of a building you switch to "flying mode" and can't jump down anymore.

Nonetheless it is a fun game. Perhaps try it when there is a discount.

There are major issues with the way this game handles on the Valve Index VR Headset, using the standard Valve Index VR controllers.

Specifically, the control stick movement.  Moving the control sticks even a little bit causes the player to glitch out and launch at high speeds in a random direction.  Sometimes you even see solid-color boxes and one of them is indeed around the player's start point.

It's basically impossible to recover if you've been glitch-launched short of force-quitting the program.  The player's hands aren't even visible in that scenario (I think they get left behind when one of these launches happens).  And if you try to bring up the menu, all you get is a flashing jumble of lines.

If you can actually get to the building with that headset and controller combination, the climbing itself functions (except you're completely unable to fall - judging from the videos, you're supposed to be able to fall).

I will consider revisiting this review if the critical glitches with the Valve Index VR headset are addressed.  Until then, I would classify this as unplayable on the Valve Index VR Headset.

game_name: Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality
review_text:
There was a YouTube series called "Rossz PC Játékok" (Bad PC Games), where our good old pal FreddyD reviewed a lot of really bad games. There was one fighting game called Underground Fighting. The dev's (Mad Hog Games) only game. Similar style, unfortunately that game made in 2007 and it was horrible, but at least it was funny in its own way. On the other hand... This one made in 2024, a recent game.
It's in the name, Criminality. But against humanity.
Yeah, I get it, meant to be trashy. But this much?...

Just for fun, I decided to try this game out under my retro XP machine too.
Pentium 4, 3GHz, 1GB RAM, Radeon 9600 Pro 128mb. 15+year old machine.
Mostly using it to test out old hardware and digitizing VHS tapes.
Guess what? Run butter-smooth on it... I don't think that this game is from 2024, feels like a "rebranded game"...
Even the engine feels similar to the earlier mentioned Underground Fighting.

At first, I was excited by the art style and unique idea, but later the game became repetitive and frustrating.

Missions: Most tasks repeat the same loop (go–talk–bring back). This quickly became boring. 
Suggestion: add more mission types, like exploration or puzzles, to keep the pace fresh.

Levels: Rooms often look the same, and the layout feels confusing. Some doors are locked in ways that break the flow, and elements like the scattered vases didn’t add much, and the execution is not the best.
Suggestion: give each area a distinct purpose and guide the player more naturally through level design.

AI & Stealth : Guards chase instantly when they see you, with no suspicion stage, and they move too fast. Hiding spots are poorly placed, often outside the player’s view, which making the stealth feel unfair. 
Designing levels around AI behavior is very challenging and requires a lot of iteration and testing to balance properly. 
Suggestion: add a suspicion phase, slow the guards slightly, and refine level layouts after testing how players interact with AI.

Story & Theme: The “Azooma” theme is fun, and an unique idea, but it didn’t feel properly explored or represented in the game. The story and characters quickly became shallow and inconsistent, which broke immersion.
Suggestion: research the concept more deeply and show it through small events, or more meaningful interactions to make it feel real.

The concept is good and culturally unique, and I respect that the team managed to complete a playable game from start to finish. But with more varied missions, smarter AI, refined level design, and a deeper look at the core theme of “Azooma” the experience could become much stronger and stand out globally.
