As big as the marketing for this game was, it didn't live up to the hype. While playing the game I encountered so many problems that one might consider small, which I as I thought the team was only made of a couple of people. You can only imagine how I felt when I saw the number of names roll down in the credits.

I am not saying the game was all bad, it had its fun moments.

The majority of the game's art style was nice, and some of the animations stood out to me like the hiding animation while picking up Amira.

Some tracks of the game's music was noticeably good, like the track in the farm levels.

The "I" frames were very forgiving at times, but it didn't feel consistent.

Some of the boss fights were fun, like the chief of jinn.

The first "bad" thing that stood out to me was the walking sounds, they are so annoying especially during chases with a big number of enemies following me.

the collision boxes don't feel right as they don't give the player any chance. such as the holes in most levels, even if you get a tiny bit too close to the hole it punishes you which can get real annoying.

the map designs are really confusing, and the game doesn't point you to where you are supposed to go except for the "North East"etc that you get from the dialogue. which can make the player feel really lost most of the time, especially when backtracking is required to finish the level.

the palm trees in the farm levels are blinding as you can't see where you are supposed to go, or where an entrance would be.


I could keep going but I don't want it to seem as if I am nitpicking.

Overall I would give the game 5/10
I do not recommend

Although Azooma Escape was developed by a full team with official funding and support in Saudi Arabia, the end result feels more like a first experimental project made by one or two beginner developers. Expectations were high, but the final product is very weak compared to the resources available.

The story is almost absent, serving only as a superficial excuse to move from one stage to another. It also contradicts the main character’s personality: someone supposedly trying to escape social life but at the same time automatically accepting side tasks and helping others. This breaks immersion and weakens the player’s motivation.

The core gameplay—stealth—is fundamentally broken. Stealth elements are few and simplistic, while the character’s high movement speed makes running away a far easier option than sneaking. Enemy AI is very basic, with no adaptive responses or evolving strategies, leaving the challenge static and repetitive.

Missions are repetitive: “go, pick up, return.” Even the ghost stage, which was supposed to add variety, barely changed the core loop. Level design doesn’t make good use of the world either: maps are linear, with lots of empty space, and offer little room for experimenting with different approaches. Character progression is nonexistent; abilities stay the same from start to finish, leaving the experience flat with no sense of growth or reward.

Strengths:
The game has a unique and humorous concept: turning excessive hospitality into a comedic threat. The cultural setting and lighthearted dialogue add a distinctive flavor, and it’s refreshing to see a Saudi-developed game attempt to bring local identity to a global platform like Steam.

Verdict:
Azooma Escape carries a bold, funny idea but fails to deliver a cohesive stealth experience. Weak AI, lack of character growth, repetitive missions, and shallow level design make the game feel limited and underdeveloped. With the level of funding and support behind it, the final result should have been much stronger.

Score: 5/10

I'll probably finish playing through at some point, because I do enjoy racing games, and more specifically Forza Horizon style open-world ones. But I was a bit bummed to find that it doesn't feel anything like a bike. You start with a single speed that lets you accelerate like crazy, sprint up hills, and otherwise fly around. Later when you unlock gears, you get... a little bit higher top speed. Otherwise it's a punishment. Hills should matter. Gears should matter. The races should slow down significantly when they're climbing. You should be able to tuck on downhills and focus on descending. And in my opinion grouping up and drafting should matter more, like a real bike race, but that's much more of a headache to implement, especially with the small sizes of the races, so I'd settle for at least more of a bicycle feel to the pace of the racing.

I really tried to like this game, but it just doesn't work for me. The drifting feels weird and stiff, like you "click" into place and can't really smoothly slide around the corner, and since most of the game is dependent on the drifiting, it just all falls apart.

The car customization is a neat idea, but most cars feel the same and I never felt like I had a need to change cars or customizable parts past the starting car.

Lastly the tracks are just awful. I'm sure it's a limitation on the 2D presentation, but most later tracks can only increase the difficulty by throwing multiple back and forth sharp corners. It's like the tracks were designed seperate from the driving mechanics and they weren't tested.

The art style is great, and the character designs are nice, but overall it's not a fun driving game, despite my multiple attempts to get into it.

I'm not sure what took me longer... actually being able to take control and race (without being able to skip through all of the waffle) - or exit the game by having to go back through everything I just had to sit through to get to race. Not for me and the pixel art doesn't translate well to larger high res screens. The game appears locked at a low res and 60fps. Its definition of pixel art is low resolution gfx rather than high res pixel art.

I played a reasonable amount of this game, and it's got a generally good game feel but it's got basically no substance.  It can feel pretty good to go around the track... the first few times, and then it's just pretty bleh.

The dev of the game is pretty disappointing, too, but even ignoring that, I can't recommend this game as it's fun for maybe the first hour.  It's too bad I waited too long to actually play and start to review it, or I'd've gotten a refund.

I really wanted to enjoy this game but the drifting mechanics just don't jive with the way I want to race.
